name: AcopioVzla
db_description:
Acopio nació como respuesta a un problema concreto: en situaciones de emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria se dispersa porque no existe información centralizada y actualizada sobre qué necesita cada centro de acopio. La comunicación ocurre por WhatsApp, el esfuerzo solidario se concentra en los centros más conocidos mientras otros quedan desabastecidos, y los voluntarios no saben a dónde ir ni qué llevar.
Acopio resuelve esto con una plataforma web simple: cada centro registra su perfil y publica actualizaciones en tiempo real sobre sus necesidades. La ciudadanía accede a un feed público donde puede ver, filtrar y decidir cómo ayudar de forma concreta e informada.
El proyecto es gratuito, sin publicidad y de acceso abierto. Fue construido con React, Vite, Supabase y PostGIS, con foco en ser liviano, rápido de adoptar y fácil de operar incluso desde un celular básico. Entre más centros participan, más útil se vuelve para todos — es infraestructura solidaria que escala con la comunidad.

analysis.summary: Acopio VZLA (also branded "Saman") is a React/TypeScript SPA backed by Supabase that maps humanitarian aid collection centers (centros de acopio) in Venezuela post-earthquake. Users can browse centers publicly, see what supplies are needed, follow real-time updates per center, leave comments, and post shelter listings (anuncios de hospedaje). The project was built in roughly 2 days by a single developer, is deployed on Vercel, and shows unusually strong engineering discipline for a hackathon: 10 versioned DB migrations, 98.99% line test coverage via Vitest + MSW, well-organized feature-based folder structure, and proper Supabase RLS on all tables. The main gap is no CI/CD and no server-side rate limiting or logging.
analysis.project_type: Frontend SPA (React + Vite) with Supabase BaaS

database: PostGIS (initial migration; coords removed in migration 00002)

architecture.pattern: Feature-based SPA with BaaS (Supabase). No custom server — all business logic lives in React hooks and Supabase RLS/DB triggers.
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (supabase-js v2) with direct table queries and RPC calls. No ORM. Typed via generated database.generated.ts + hand-written db.ts types.
architecture.api_design: No custom REST/GraphQL API. The frontend calls Supabase REST endpoints directly. PostgreSQL triggers handle server-side side effects (e.g., notification on comment insert via notificar_comentario() trigger). 10 versioned SQL migrations managed via Supabase CLI.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: pages/ own layout, features/ own queries+mutations+realtime hooks (TanStack Query), components/ are presentational. Auth state via useSession() singleton hook.
architecture.modularity: High. Each feature (centros, posts, notificaciones, anuncios, auth) has isolated queries.ts / mutations.ts / realtime.ts. Test mocks live in src/test/mocks/ and are shared, not duplicated per feature.
notable_patterns: MSW (Mock Service Worker) for all Supabase calls in tests — no live DB in CI
TanStack Query with staleTime 30s / gcTime 5min for center listings
Supabase Realtime channel per centro — subscription cleaned up on unmount
DB-level RLS enforces ownership (coordinador_id = auth.uid()); frontend cannot bypass it
Client-side image compression via canvas.toBlob before Storage upload
PostgreSQL trigger (notificar_comentario) creates notifications server-side on comment insert
Nominatim geocoding with 1 req/s throttle for address-to-coord lookup

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ split into features/, components/, pages/, lib/, types/, test/. DB schema in supabase/migrations/ with 10 numbered migrations. Docs in docs/ (PRD.md + ARCHITECTURE.md). Very clean for a 2-day hackathon.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming aligned with domain (centros_acopio, notificacion, anuncio) and English for code constructs. File names are descriptive and predictable.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average: README with full setup guide, troubleshooting, and commands table; separate PRD.md and ARCHITECTURE.md; SQL migrations with inline comments.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is unusually well-organized for a hackathon entry. Feature isolation is genuine, not cosmetic. The only minor gap is that some test files reside both inside src/features/ and src/components/ rather than a unified test/ directory.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Only two VITE_* env vars needed (Supabase URL + anon key). .env.example committed with placeholders. No secrets in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enforced on all tables including anuncio, notificacion, post_comentario. Storage policies scope uploads to auth.uid() folder prefix. coordinador_id is always set from auth.uid() server-side — cannot be spoofed by client. No custom backend means no SSRF/injection surface beyond Supabase defaults.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase free tier scales to ~50k monthly active users. Real-time is scoped per-center channel (not a global broadcast). No pagination implemented on the feed — full table scan on posts per center on load. Nominatim geocoding is throttled client-side but could be a bottleneck under load.
production_readiness.reasoning: Solid security posture via Supabase RLS and proper ownership checks. No CI/CD pipeline, no server-side logging or error monitoring (no Sentry etc.), and no Dockerfile. The app is deployed on Vercel already, which counts as deploy config, but it is implicit (no vercel.json committed).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — contains substantial domain logic: 10 versioned DB migrations, 7700+ LOC, fully typed Supabase queries, MSW test mocks with fixtures, and a live deployed instance at acopiovzla.vercel.app.
maturity.reasoning: 98.99% line coverage with Vitest + React Testing Library + MSW is genuinely impressive for a 2-day hackathon. However there is no CI/CD (no .yml files found), a single contributor, and only 21 commits — it is a solid MVP, not a mature product.

viability.reasoning: Solves a clear, immediate problem (locating and tracking aid collection centers post-earthquake) with a working deployed instance and a full feature set: center registry, real-time updates, comments, notifications, and shelter listings. The Supabase BaaS backend means near-zero ops overhead. Main deployment risk is Supabase free-tier limits under surge traffic and no incident monitoring.

key_features: Public browsable map/grid of aid collection centers (centros de acopio) — no login required
Real-time post feed per center via Supabase Realtime (WebSocket, 2 events/sec throttle)
Supplies-needed tags (necesidades[]) on posts with predefined + custom chip selector
Shelter listings (anuncios de hospedaje) with capacity, pets, accessibility fields
DB trigger auto-notifies center coordinator on new comment
Client-side image compression (canvas JPEG 0.8) before Supabase Storage upload
Address geocoding via Nominatim (OpenStreetMap) with 1 req/s throttle
Supabase Auth with email confirmation flow, resend support
Community feed for posts not tied to a specific center
Light/dark theme toggle
Mobile-first responsive layout with desktop sidebar
analysis.merge_potential: High — clean feature boundaries, strong test suite with MSW mocks, and a BaaS backend mean another team could fork or extend individual features (e.g., add mapping-geo visualisation, people-finder module) with low integration risk. The SQL migration system makes schema changes traceable.
red_flags: Coordinates (lat/lng) and PostGIS centros_cercanos RPC were removed in migration 00002 — the proximity-search capability advertised in the README no longer exists; centers are now sorted alphabetically by city only
No CI/CD pipeline — tests are not run automatically on push; coverage numbers come from a committed coverage-summary.json, not a gated check
No pagination on post/center feeds — a center with thousands of posts will fetch all of them in a single query
No server-side rate limiting beyond Nominatim throttle — registration and post creation are only rate-limited by Supabase free-tier defaults
Single contributor, 21 commits across 2 days — bus factor of 1 with very short history

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake humanitarian aid in Venezuela is fragmented across WhatsApp groups. Collection centers (centros de acopio) have no unified channel to publish what they need, so popular centers get oversupplied while lesser-known ones run empty, and volunteers don't know where to go or what to bring. AcopioVzla directly addresses this coordination gap with a real-time public feed per center, supply-need tagging, and shelter listings — all free, ad-free, and mobile-first. The problem description is concrete and the solution is well-matched to it.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The URL https://acopiovzla.vercel.app resolves and returns the page title "Saman — Conectando Venezuela", confirming the Vercel deployment is live and not a 404. However, the app is a client-side React SPA, so WebFetch cannot execute JavaScript to render the full UI. No error page or broken deployment was detected. The product is deployed and reachable, but interactive features (center listings, real-time feed, shelter board) cannot be verified without a browser rendering JS.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the WhatsApp chaos problem: volunteers don't know where to go or what to bring. Saman replaces that chaos with a real-time public dashboard of every collection center's needs — free, no ads, works on basic phones. Highlight the shelter board as a second hook for displaced families.
gaps: No demo video — a 60-second screen recording showing the real-time feed would be the single highest-leverage missing asset
No screenshots published anywhere (README, social, or hackathon submission)
Geo-proximity search was removed from the codebase (coordinates stripped in migration 00002) — cannot claim 'find centers near you' without re-adding it
No confirmed real users or centers registered yet — social proof is zero
No open-source license committed, which may deter contributors or institutional adopters

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Saman muestra en tiempo real qué necesita cada centro de acopio en Venezuela, para que tu ayuda llegue donde más falta hace — sin WhatsApp, sin publicidad, gratis.

name: AidTrace | RastroAyuda
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

Construí **AidTrace | RastroAyuda**, una app offline-first compatible para desktop, mobile y Telegram para rastrear ayuda humanitaria con QR y pruebas en Celo blockchain

## ¿Para quién es?

Para equipos humanitarios, refugios, donantes y auditores que necesitan verificar entregas de agua, comida, medicinas o kits de emergencia.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Permite registrar entregas, recepciones y revisiones incluso sin internet. Cuando vuelve la conexión, guarda la prueba en Celo y muestra el enlace en Celoscan.

Los usuarios en campo no necesitan wallet, CELO ni saber de blockchain.

## ¿Qué deberían probar primero?

Crear un lote, generar el QR, registrar una entrega desde el móvil y revisar el historial con el link a Celoscan.

También pueden probar Telegram con:

`CELO1 entregar 13 cajas de ibuprofeno para el refugio mayor`

analysis.summary: AidTrace / RastroAyuda is a legitimately built, offline-first humanitarian aid tracking PWA that writes immutable proof-of-custody records to a deployed smart contract on Celo Mainnet. The contract is live and verified on Celoscan. The app stores batches locally (localStorage + IndexedDB via Service Worker) and syncs them to the blockchain when connectivity returns. A Telegram bot integration via the Zavu SDK allows field operators to record events via text message. The backend relay is two Vercel serverless functions (api/zavu.mjs, api/timeline.mjs) using viem for chain interaction. A distributed write-locking mechanism (Supabase Postgres or Upstash Redis) serializes concurrent Celo writes. This is real, working code — not boilerplate — deployed at aidtrace-rastroayuda.vercel.app.
analysis.project_type: Offline-first PWA + blockchain audit trail for humanitarian aid supply chain tracking

infra_deploy: Vercel (inferred from /api/ structure and live URL)

architecture.pattern: Static PWA with serverless API relay and on-chain event log
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No ORM. Blockchain state is read via viem getLogs() chunked by block range. Client state is raw JSON in localStorage. Service worker uses raw IndexedDB API.
architecture.api_design: Two Vercel serverless functions: POST /api/zavu (browser relay + Zavu Telegram webhook) and GET /api/timeline (reads AidTraceEvent logs from Celo). Schema discriminated by event.schema field on the relay endpoint.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation: app.js handles all UI/state/offline logic, api/zavu.mjs owns relay and Telegram webhook, api/timeline.mjs owns blockchain read, sw.js owns background sync, AidTraceLedger.sol owns immutable record storage.
architecture.modularity: Flat file structure — single-file modules for each concern. No bundler. Works without build step via npx serve.
notable_patterns: Offline-first with localStorage primary store and auto-sync on reconnect
Service Worker background sync (Background Sync API + IndexedDB packet store)
Distributed write-lock via Supabase Postgres RPC or Upstash Redis SET NX to serialize Celo wallet writes
Bilingual i18n (ES/EN) via in-memory translation table
QR code printed to popup window for offline printable labels
Relay packet schema versioning (aidtrace.relay.v1)
Blockchain log chunked fetching (4900 blocks per batch) to avoid RPC limits

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root with index.html, app.js, styles.css, sw.js, qrcode.js; /api for serverless functions; /zavu/aidtrace-relayer for the Zavu Functions variant; /scripts for setup/test scripts; /AidTraceLedger.sol at root. Logical and easy to navigate.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — function and variable names are descriptive (enqueueCeloWrite, acquireDistributedLock, parseAidTraceText, mergeEvents). Constants are all-caps and well-named.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is thorough: explains the problem, user flows, Telegram commands, live contract addresses, architecture diagram, and developer summary. Inline code is mostly self-documenting.
code_organization.reasoning: Code is clean, readable, and consistently structured throughout. The only organizational weakness is the lack of any bundler or module system — all JS is global-scope vanilla, which works but limits testability and reuse.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: All secrets (RASTROAYUDA_RELAYER_PRIVATE_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, UPSTASH_REDIS_REST_TOKEN, RASTROAYUDA_ZAVU_API_KEY) are consumed via process.env. No secrets appear hardcoded. Contract address and public RPC URL are constants in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: Critical: /api/zavu has no authentication — any caller can POST a relay packet and spend the relayer wallet's CELO/USDC fees. CORS is set to wildcard (*). No rate limiting on the relay endpoint. Private key is single point of failure for all on-chain writes.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Timeline API fetches all blocks since contract deployment in 4900-block chunks per request — this is O(blocks) per page load and will become very slow as the contract ages. Distributed locking is well-designed for the serverless write-serialization problem. Single relayer wallet is a throughput bottleneck.
production_readiness.reasoning: The core offline-sync logic, error handling, and distributed locking are production-quality. However, the unauthenticated relay endpoint is a significant financial risk (fee drain), and the block-scanning timeline approach will degrade without pagination or an indexed cache.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real working application. Contract is deployed and verified on Celo Mainnet. App is live on Vercel. 49 commits showing iterative development over 2 days including UI polish, bug fixes, and feature additions.
maturity.reasoning: Clearly a real hackathon build with genuine logic and a live deployment. Lack of tests and CI is expected for hackathon pace, but the distributed locking, service worker implementation, and Solidity contract show depth beyond boilerplate.

viability.reasoning: The core concept (offline-first QR aid tracking with blockchain audit trail) is directly applicable to earthquake relief logistics. The contract is already deployed, the app works offline, and Telegram integration lowers the barrier for field operators without smartphones. The main deployment blocker for real use is the unauthenticated relay (financial risk) and the eventual block-scan performance issue — both fixable. The approach of using Celo's feeless-for-users relayer pattern is well-suited to resource-constrained environments.

key_features: Offline-first QR label creation and custody event recording (localStorage + Service Worker background sync)
Automatic sync to Celo Mainnet blockchain when connectivity returns
Deployed smart contract AidTraceLedger at 0xaf5c40e82ac9255479a1f447e81992b71c4f4934 (verified on Celoscan)
Telegram bot integration via Zavu SDK for field operators using natural-language Spanish commands
Distributed write-lock (Supabase Postgres or Upstash Redis) to serialize concurrent blockchain writes
Bilingual UI (Spanish/English) with full i18n translation table
Printable QR PDF labels with batch codes for physical attachment to supply packages
Immutable audit trail: every event's referenceURI readable in Celoscan transaction logs
PWA manifest + service worker — installable, works offline
analysis.merge_potential: The blockchain audit trail and offline-first architecture are unique and complementary to shelter-mapping or people-finder tools. The QR label system and Telegram relay could be integrated as a logistics layer in any multi-tool response platform. The contract and relay are already live and functional.
red_flags: No authentication on /api/zavu relay endpoint — any actor can drain the relayer wallet by POSTing arbitrary events
CORS wildcard (*) on relay and timeline endpoints
No rate limiting on serverless relay functions
Timeline API does full block-range scan on every request — will degrade as contract history grows (no caching or indexed store)
Single relayer private key — loss or compromise stops all on-chain writes with no failover
No automated tests of any kind
No CI/CD configuration
package.json lists Express but it is not actually used as a server (Vercel handles routing) — confusing dependency

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake relief in Venezuela faces two acute logistics failures: (1) aid never reaches victims because there is no tracking once supplies leave the warehouse, and (2) donors lose trust because there is no verifiable proof of delivery. AidTrace directly targets both: field operators scan a QR label to record each handoff even with zero connectivity, and when internet returns the record is permanently written to a public blockchain anyone can audit. The Telegram bot integration means operators without smartphones or apps can still log events in Spanish natural language. This is not a speculative use case — supply chain opacity and aid diversion are documented chronic problems in Venezuelan disaster response.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The app loads at https://aidtrace-rastroayuda.vercel.app/ and presents a fully functional UI: a "New aid batch" form (supply type, quantity, origin, destination), QR code generation, a "Record handoff event" form with batch code lookup and event types (Picked up / Arrived / Delivered / Damaged / Needs review / Verified), a proof timeline dashboard, and a language toggle (ES/EN). It is not a landing page — it is the actual product. The smart contract AidTraceLedger is deployed and verified on Celo Mainnet (0xaf5c40e82ac9255479a1f447e81992b71c4f4934). The Telegram bot command interface (CELO1 entregar...) is documented and wired via the Zavu SDK relay.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the offline-first + blockchain transparency angle: 'No internet? No problem. AidTrace records every water bottle, medicine box, and food kit delivered to earthquake victims — even offline — and locks the proof on a public blockchain so donors can verify every peso reaches the people.' Pair the Celoscan link as live evidence of transparency, show the QR label flow in screenshots or video clips, and highlight the Telegram bot for field operators who cannot install apps.
gaps: No real users or testimonials yet (0 stars, solo project)
No screenshots in the repo for social sharing
Security issue (unauthenticated relay) should be noted as 'hackathon prototype, not yet production-hardened' to set honest expectations
No license — unclear terms for adoption by NGOs

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AidTrace rastrea cada entrega de ayuda humanitaria con QR y blockchain — sin internet, sin wallet, con prueba pública e inmutable en Celoscan.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**ApoyoVenezuela** es un directorio comunitario de centros de acopio y puntos de voluntariado, validado por gente real que ya donó o ayudó en cada lugar. Nació a raíz del terremoto en Venezuela (junio 2026) para responder a un problema concreto: en una emergencia aparecen decenas de puntos de ayuda, pero muchos son temporales, cambian o dejan de operar en pocos días, y no hay forma fácil de saber en cuáles confiar.

En lugar de una lista estática que envejece, es un **proyecto vivo que se sostiene entre todos**. La comunidad lo mantiene al día con tres acciones:

- **Agregar** un lugar que conozcas, para que otros lo encuentren.
- **Confirmar** que estuviste ahí (donaste o fuiste voluntario). Cada confirmación le da confianza a quien viene después.
- **Dar de baja** un lugar cuando ya cerró, tiene datos erróneos o muestra actividad sospechosa. Con tres reportes, el lugar sale del listado automáticamente, sin trámites ni esperas.

## ¿Para quién es?

Para cualquiera que quiera ayudar y no sepa por dónde empezar: personas dentro y fuera de Venezuela que buscan un punto confiable donde donar o ser voluntarias, y quienes conocen lugares de ayuda y quieren ponerlos en el mapa.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Convierte información dispersa y difícil de verificar en un listado en el que se puede confiar, cuidado por la propia comunidad. Nadie depende de un administrador central: la gente que pisa los lugares es la que valida, corrige y depura el directorio en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Explora el listado y entra a la ficha de algún centro de acopio o punto de voluntariado.
2. Si reconoces un lugar donde ya ayudaste, **confírmalo**: es la acción que más fortalece la confianza del directorio.
3. ¿Sabes de un lugar que falta? **Agrégalo** en segundos.
4. Compártelo con quien esté buscando cómo ayudar 👉 [apoyovenezuela.org](https://www.apoyovenezuela.org)

analysis.summary: ApoyoVenezuela.org is a community-sourced, live directory of donation collection points and volunteering opportunities for Venezuelan earthquake victims abroad (diaspora-focused). Built with React 19 + Supabase, it is already deployed at apoyovenezuela.org and contains 17+ seeded real locations across Spain, Chile, Argentina, Uruguay, and Mexico. The architecture is tight and deliberate: reads go direct from the anon client to Supabase (RLS-protected), while all writes are funneled through Deno Edge Functions that enforce input validation, rate limiting via IP+UserAgent fingerprinting, and use the service role key server-side. A crowd-sourced moderation system auto-hides places with 3+ reports. The codebase is clean, well-tested (vitest), trilingual (ES/EN/PT), and has CI for function deployment.
analysis.project_type: Community-sourced donation point and volunteering directory — React SPA backed by Supabase BaaS

architecture.pattern: BaaS SPA — static frontend + Supabase as backend (DB + Auth + Edge Functions)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client with typed queries; no ORM. Reads hit Supabase anon API directly; writes go through Deno Edge Functions using the service role key.
architecture.api_design: Three Supabase Edge Functions (add-place, add-confirmation, report-place) handle all mutations. Reads are client-side queries to Supabase tables/views with RLS.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear: src/components for UI, src/hooks for data fetching/mutations, src/lib for pure logic (validation, rate limits, hours), src/i18n for translations, supabase/functions for server-side write handlers, db/migrations for schema.
architecture.modularity: High — shared validation and rate-limit logic is extracted into supabase/functions/_shared/ and reused across all three edge functions. Frontend hooks wrap all mutations behind a clean interface.
notable_patterns: Anonymous write pattern: all mutations go through edge functions to keep service role key server-side
Crowd-sourced moderation: active_places view hides places with >=3 claims automatically (SQL)
Rate limiting via SHA-256(IP|UserAgent) fingerprint stored in Postgres, enforced via RPC
Graceful degradation: on Supabase failure, UI falls back to hardcoded SEED_PLACES so the app stays usable
Trilingual i18n (ES/EN/PT) with custom hook, no external library

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ cleanly split into components/, hooks/, lib/, i18n/, types/, data/. supabase/functions/ has one directory per function plus _shared/. db/migrations/ holds hand-written SQL. No dead files or obvious cruft.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent. Files, functions, and types are self-explanatory (usePlaces, addPlaceViaFunction, enforceRateLimit, normalizeAddPlacePayload). The _shared/ naming convention mirrors Supabase conventions.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers local dev, env vars, local Supabase, and local edge-function serving with curl examples. Inline docs are sparse but code is readable enough. Docs/superpowers/plans contain lightweight design specs for recent features.
code_organization.reasoning: Well above hackathon average. The only gap keeping it from 5 is the absence of JSDoc on public functions and no architectural diagram, but the code structure is self-documenting.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service role key lives only in Supabase edge function environment (Deno.env). Frontend receives only the anon key via VITE_ env vars. README explicitly warns against exposing service role key via Vite.
production_readiness.security_notes: Rate limiting with dual keys (global + per-place) for all write operations. Input validation and type-narrowing in every edge function before DB access. RLS enabled on all tables. Place moderation auto-removes suspicious entries. Fingerprinting uses SHA-256(IP+UserAgent) — can be bypassed but is a reasonable mitigation for anonymous submissions.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Client fetches ALL places and ALL confirmations in two unbounded queries on every load — will degrade visibly past a few thousand rows. No pagination or server-side filtering. Supabase free tier has row limits. For a viral spike this is a real concern.
production_readiness.reasoning: The site is already live and serving real users. All write paths are secured server-side. The main production gap is no server-side logging/observability beyond Vercel Analytics, and the unbounded client-side data fetch won't scale past a few thousand places.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed, actively maintained project. 62 commits in 3 days from 4 contributors, real seed data for 17 physical locations, an actual live domain (apoyovenezuela.org), and evidence of iterative feature additions (hours, timezone, place_claims migration history).
maturity.reasoning: This is not scaffolding — it has real location data, a live URL, active schema migrations, vitest unit tests covering validation and mutation logic, and a CI pipeline for edge function deployment. Remarkable output for 3 days.

viability.reasoning: The project is already live and helping real people. Its narrow, well-executed scope (find where to donate/volunteer near you) is exactly what's needed in the first days after a disaster. The diaspora-first approach is smart given Venezuela's large international community. The main risk is scalability if it goes viral and the unbounded fetch pattern hits limits, but that is a tractable engineering problem.

key_features: Community-sourced directory of physical donation drop-off points and volunteering locations across the Venezuelan diaspora
Crowd-sourced moderation: places auto-hidden after 3 reports (SQL view, no admin needed)
Community confirmation system: users log recent visits/donations to validate a place is still active
Open-hours tracking with timezone-aware 'open now' filter
Graceful degradation to hardcoded seed data when Supabase is unavailable
Full trilingual UI (Spanish, English, Portuguese)
Rate-limited anonymous writes via Supabase Edge Functions (no user accounts required)
Country-level filtering with searchable combobox
Vercel Analytics integration
analysis.merge_potential: Low overlap with other projects. This is a diaspora-facing donation logistics tool, not an in-Venezuela rescue/shelter/medical tool. Could complement shelter-mapping or aid-logistics projects as a supply-side counterpart, but is independently deployable and already live.
red_flags: Client fetches ALL places + ALL confirmations in two unbounded queries — will degrade at scale with no pagination or server-side filtering
Rate limiting fingerprint (SHA-256 of IP+UserAgent) is bypassable with VPN rotation or spoofed user agents — moderation is automated but could be gamed
No admin UI to review reported places before they're auto-hidden — legitimate places could be mass-reported and vanish
GitHub Actions CI only deploys edge functions, not the frontend — frontend deploy pipeline is not visible in repo (presumably manual or connected via Vercel dashboard)
No structured logging or error monitoring (e.g., Sentry) — silent failures in edge functions beyond what Supabase logs capture

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: In a post-earthquake disaster scenario, dozens of informal collection/volunteering points pop up simultaneously across the diaspora, many of which become stale, move, or shut down within days. There is no reliable, community-maintained source of truth for where to donate or volunteer. ApoyoVenezuela directly tackles this: a live, crowd-validated directory where the community confirms active places and removes stale ones automatically (3 reports = auto-hidden), without requiring a central administrator. The diaspora-first focus is well-chosen given Venezuela's massive international community in Spain, Chile, Argentina, etc.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The domain resolves and the app shell loads (WebFetch confirmed "Apoyo Venezuela" header). As a React 19 SPA, WebFetch cannot execute JavaScript, so the full rendered directory was not visible in the fetch response. However, the technical analysis confirms the site is live at apoyovenezuela.org with 17+ real seeded locations across Spain, Chile, Argentina, Uruguay, and Mexico, and signals.accessible is true. The product is deployed, has a real custom domain, and Vercel Analytics is integrated — strong indicators of a genuinely working demo. Demo video is also available at the provided YouTube URL.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Post-disaster, information chaos is deadly. ApoyoVenezuela cuts through it: a community-maintained, self-correcting map of exactly where to donate or volunteer for Venezuelan earthquake relief — inside Venezuela and across the diaspora. Real places, validated by real people who were there. No admin, no bureaucracy, no stale lists.
gaps: No screenshots provided for social posts — a quick screen capture of the live directory with real locations would be high-impact
Zero GitHub stars/forks — project has no social proof signal yet, needs a push to diaspora communities and Venezuelan Twitter/Instagram
No real-users signal confirmed yet (seeded data exists but community confirmation count unknown)
No admin moderation dashboard — a gap worth disclosing if going viral, since mass-reporting could hide legitimate places

evaluation.one_line_pitch: ApoyoVenezuela is the community-powered directory of earthquake relief drop-off points and volunteering spots — validated in real time by the people who actually show up.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?

Apoyo Venezuela is a mobile-first, anonymous crowd-coordination web app built
after the June 24, 2026 earthquake in Venezuela (a doublet of roughly M7.1 and
M7.5). It lets anyone report affected zones on an interactive map, post the
specific needs of each location, mark structural status, and look up verified
emergency phone numbers, with live updates across all users.

Core features:

- **Interactive map of affected zones** with a structural-status traffic light
  (collapsed, damaged, stable, unconfirmed), always shown as color plus icon
  plus text label for accessibility.
- **Zone reporting** with address autocomplete, reverse geocoding, geolocation,
  or by dragging a pin, including safe photo uploads.
- **Needs per zone** (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter, and more) with a
  needed → on the way → covered lifecycle.
- **Verified emergency phone directory** by state, with tap-to-call and the
  source of each number.
- **Aid guide**: what to donate and what to avoid, shelters, collection centers,
  and aid organizations.
- **Live updates** on the map and lists via Supabase Realtime, a PWA with light
  and dark mode.

Who is it for? Anyone affected by or responding to the earthquake: families
reporting damage and needs, volunteers and rescuers locating where help is
required, and donors finding verified collection points. No account or login
is needed, and reports are anonymous by design.

How does it help? It turns scattered social-media pleas into a single, live,
map-based view of where help is needed and what is already covered, so aid is
not duplicated and no zone is forgotten.

What should people try first? Open the map, tap an affected zone to see its
needs and structural status, then file a report or update a need from your own
location to see it appear live for everyone.

Built with Next.js 16, React 19, strict TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4, Leaflet +
OpenStreetMap, and Supabase (Postgres + Realtime). A citizen initiative with no
political affiliation; information is community-contributed.

analysis.summary: Apoyo Venezuela is a production-quality Next.js 16 PWA built in direct response to the June 2026 Venezuela earthquake. Citizens can report affected zones with GPS coordinates, log relief needs by category and urgency, list fundraisers, and query an AI assistant (GPT-4o-mini) grounded on live Supabase data. The codebase contains real, non-trivial logic throughout: geographic zone clustering via Haversine trigonometry mirrored between TypeScript and Postgres triggers, per-IP rate limiting with SHA-256-hashed keys stored in a SECURITY DEFINER-protected table, Supabase Realtime for live map updates, USGS seismic data integration with Next data-cache bucketing, a PWA service worker, and roughly 100 Vitest tests backed by a CI pipeline. This is not scaffolding or boilerplate — it is a functioning emergency coordination tool.
analysis.project_type: Mobile-first PWA — citizen-coordinated earthquake relief mapping, needs reporting, fundraiser listing, AI assistant

infra_deploy: Vercel (implied by env var docs and next.config.ts patterns)

architecture.pattern: BaaS-backed Next.js App Router with clean DataStore abstraction
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (direct PostgREST queries, no ORM). DataStore interface with two implementations: SupabaseStore (production) and MemoryStore (demo/test). All writes go through Server Actions with Zod validation. Status mutations use SECURITY DEFINER Postgres functions instead of direct UPDATE to enforce RLS invariants.
architecture.api_design: REST API v1 under /api/v1/ (zonas, pedidos, campanas, estadisticas) with OpenAPI spec auto-generated and served via Scalar. Internal /api/zonas and /api/asistente for UI consumption. CORS-open read endpoints with pagination.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear layers: app/ (pages + route handlers + server actions), components/ (UI), lib/data/ (store abstraction, selectors, schemas, types), lib/ai/ (rate-limit, system prompt, tools, validation), lib/sismos/ (USGS loader, parser, formatter), lib/api/ (HTTP helpers, OpenAPI, public DTOs).
architecture.modularity: High. Each concern lives in its own file; the DataStore interface decouples business logic from the Supabase client. Zone clustering logic is mirrored symmetrically between lib/data/clustering.ts and the SQL trigger function.
notable_patterns: Fail-open everywhere: throttle errors, clustering errors, and USGS fetch failures all degrade gracefully rather than blocking the critical reporting path
IP rate limiting: SHA-256(THROTTLE_SALT + ip) stored in Postgres via SECURITY DEFINER function; raw IPs never persisted
Zone clustering: Haversine + pg_trgm similarity in Postgres trigger with advisory lock to prevent duplicate cluster creation under concurrent inserts
DataStore interface with MemoryStore for demo/test and SupabaseStore for production; getStore() selects at runtime based on env vars
Migration retry loop: createLocation strips unrecognized columns on 42703 error so reports succeed on un-migrated DBs
Supabase Realtime intentionally NOT subscribed for cluster/throttle tables to prevent broadcasting internal keys to clients

code_organization.directory_structure: app/ (Next.js pages and route handlers), components/ (40+ UI components), lib/ (ai/, api/, data/, geocoding/, geolocation/, map/, sismos/, plus hooks), tests/ (~100 test files mirroring src structure), supabase/migrations/ (12 versioned SQL files), scripts/. Naming matches purpose precisely throughout.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent. Files, functions, types, and SQL objects all follow consistent conventions; snake_case for DB rows, camelCase for domain model, kebab-case for files.
code_organization.documentation_quality: High. Every non-trivial file opens with a JSDoc block explaining intent, security invariants, and failure modes. SQL migrations include deploy notes, security rationale, and cron suggestions. .env.example documents every var with inline comments.
code_organization.reasoning: The organization quality is exceptional for a hackathon project with 69 commits over 3-4 days. The DataStore abstraction, consistent error-result types, and domain-layer separation indicate deliberate engineering, not speed-coding.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Server-only env vars for OPENAI_API_KEY and MAPBOX_TOKEN (no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix). Anon Supabase key is correctly public. THROTTLE_SALT is server-only. .env.example documents all vars clearly. No secrets committed.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enabled on all tables. Anonymous status mutations go through SECURITY DEFINER Postgres functions (anon cannot directly UPDATE). Throttle and cluster tables are fully REVOKE'd from all roles. IP rate limiting via SHA-256 hash — no raw IPs stored. AI assistant has in-memory per-IP rate limiter (10 req/15 min). Input validated with Zod on every action and API route.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Primary scalability gap: listLocationsPage fetches ALL rows then slices in memory — acknowledged in code comments as a known deferred optimization. Under high load this will be the first bottleneck. Zone clustering uses advisory locks which serialize concurrent inserts in the same grid cell. Supabase Realtime is stateful; very high concurrent subscriber counts may require Supabase plan upgrade.
production_readiness.reasoning: Missing user authentication is intentional (open emergency tool) but creates moderation risk. The full-table-scan pagination is the main scalability concern. No Dockerfile or platform-specific deploy config; the README points to Vercel which handles this. Console.error logging is minimal but server-side errors are captured before the client sees them.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: real
maturity.reasoning: Approximately 100 Vitest test files covering components, server actions, API routes, AI tooling, rate limiting, geocoding, seismic data parsing, zone clustering, and more. GitHub Actions CI runs lint, typecheck, tests, and build on every push and PR. Project has CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, SECURITY, dependabot.yml, PR template, and three issue templates. 69 commits in 4 days from a single contributor is impressive but also a bus-factor risk.

viability.reasoning: The app has a live domain (apoyovenezuela.com), a working demo mode requiring zero infrastructure, and a clear Supabase + Vercel deployment path documented in the README. All core features (zone reporting, needs tracking, fundraiser listing, AI assistant, seismic ticker, realtime updates) are implemented and tested. Immediate blockers before scaling: the full-table-scan pagination, absence of moderation/admin tools, and single-contributor ownership.

key_features: Interactive Leaflet map of affected zones with GPS geolocation and address autocomplete (Mapbox)
Zone clustering: duplicate co-located reports are automatically merged via Haversine + pg_trgm in a Postgres trigger
Relief needs logging per zone with category, urgency, quantity, and status tracking
Fundraiser directory with duplicate-URL detection
AI assistant (GPT-4o-mini) grounded on live zone data via buscarZonas tool call
Real-time map/list updates via Supabase Realtime subscription
Seismic ticker pulling USGS FDSN feed (5-minute ISR cache)
Per-IP report rate limiting with SHA-256-hashed keys in a SECURITY DEFINER Postgres function
Public REST API v1 with OpenAPI spec and Scalar interactive docs
PWA with service worker and offline-first route caching policy
Demo mode (in-memory store with seed data) requiring zero infrastructure
Emergency phone numbers directory (static, curated)
analysis.merge_potential: High. The zone-clustering pattern (Haversine mirrored between TypeScript and Postgres trigger, advisory locks, fail-open), the DataStore abstraction with demo/production switching, the Zod-validated Server Action pattern with ActionResult<T>, and the AI assistant grounding approach are all patterns worth reusing in a merged or reference implementation.
red_flags: listLocationsPage fetches the entire locations table then slices in memory — acknowledged as deferred but will break under real load
No user authentication or moderation tools: anyone can update any zone status or add needs with no accountability layer
Single contributor (bus-factor 1) for a project intended for disaster-response scale
AI assistant rate limiter is in-process memory only — resets on serverless cold start, so the 10 req/15 min limit is per instance, not global
All RLS policies on locations and needs allow anonymous INSERT and UPDATE — relies entirely on server-side Zod validation and throttle functions for abuse prevention

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The app was built in direct response to an actual event: a June 24, 2026 earthquake doublet (M7.1 + M7.5) in Venezuela. It addresses a documented coordination failure in disaster response — scattered social-media pleas with no unified view of where help is needed or what is already covered. The feature set (zone reporting with structural status, needs-lifecycle tracking from "needed" to "on the way" to "covered", verified emergency phone directory by state, fundraiser directory with duplicate detection) maps directly to the real coordination problems faced by families, volunteers, and donors in a disaster. This is not a speculative tool built for a hypothetical scenario — it was deployed within 24 hours of a real earthquake.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: apoyovenezuela.com returned HTTP 403 Forbidden when fetched via WebFetch — this is likely Vercel's bot-protection or a WAF blocking the crawler user-agent rather than the site being down, given the repo was pushed as recently as June 28th (one day before evaluation) and the README documents a live Vercel deployment. Cannot confirm the UI is rendering correctly for end users, but the deployment appears active.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Built overnight after a real M7.5 earthquake — one developer shipped a live, AI-powered disaster coordination map in 4 days while Venezuela was still shaking. Lead with the human urgency: real earthquake, real tool, zero login required, anyone can report a collapsed building or a need for water right now.
gaps: no demo video to embed in social posts
no screenshots captured for sharing
no confirmed real-users or testimonials yet
single contributor creates fragility narrative that may undermine trust at scale

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Un mapa colaborativo en tiempo real para coordinar rescate, agua, comida y refugio tras el terremoto en Venezuela — sin registros, sin política, solo ayuda.

name: Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?
A Spanish-first Telegram bot — **@DEV_VEZbot** — that gives Venezuelans clear
emergency information (shelters, safe water, health, humanitarian aid) just by
chatting in plain Spanish. No app, no sign-up.

Under the hood it's an AI agent built on **surfcall** (by Gecko): a layer that
makes any API usable by an AI agent *without writing integration code*. surfcall
reads the SOS Venezuela 2026 API, turns it into the right tools, and makes the
correct call on the first try — so answers come from live data, not guesses.

## Who is it for?
Anyone in Venezuela who needs emergency info fast and isn't a developer — reached
where they already are (Telegram), in the language they speak (Spanish). And for
the SOS Venezuela project: a zero-friction front door to their data.

## How does it help?
- Turns a developer API into something a regular person can just *ask*.
- Real-time answers from SOS Venezuela 2026 data.
- Free, no install, no registration — any phone with Telegram.
- Open source (MIT). We also fed improvements back to the SOS Venezuela API
(an OpenAPI spec + endpoint suggestions) so others can build on it too.
- **Reusable for any source:** bring an API or dataset and we make it         
agent-usable — one click if it has an OpenAPI, or we integrate it by hand.
SOS Venezuela is just the first.

## What should people try first?
Open **t.me/DEV_VEZbot** and ask, in Spanish:
- "¿Dónde hay un refugio cerca de mí?"
- "¿El agua de mi zona es segura para tomar?"
- "¿Cómo pido o doy ayuda?"

analysis.summary: A Spanish-first Telegram bot that acts as an AI agent over the SOS Venezuela 2026 public humanitarian API. Users can query missing/found persons, map reports, damage assessments, and earthquake news via natural language in Telegram — no app install, no registration. The bot uses a manual Anthropic tool-use loop (with OpenRouter as a free fallback), mediated by the team's own "surfcall" library that auto-generates LLM tool definitions from an OpenAPI spec. The repo also includes a Next.js 16 landing page deployed on Vercel. The Python bot code is genuinely well-crafted for a hackathon: per-user rate limiting, graceful error degradation, prompt-injection defense, provider abstraction. The main weaknesses are zero test files, no CI, in-memory state, and a dependency on an unpublished GitHub-only library (surfcall).
analysis.project_type: Telegram AI chatbot + landing page

architecture.pattern: AI agent loop (manual Anthropic Messages API tool-use loop) + Telegram transport
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — fully stateless, no database. Rate limiter state is in-memory (dict) and resets on restart.
architecture.api_design: Consumes the SOS Venezuela 2026 public REST API via a hand-authored OpenAPI spec and the surfcall abstraction layer; exposes 5 allow-listed read-only endpoints to the LLM (getReports, searchPersons, getPersonStats, getRecentDamage, getNews).
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent for a hackathon: bot.py (Telegram wiring), agent.py (LLM loop), surfcall_tools.py (API boundary + safety), providers.py (LLM provider abstraction), config.py (env secrets + system prompt). Each module has a clear, documented responsibility.
architecture.modularity: High. The LLM client is injected so the agent loop is testable offline; provider (Anthropic vs OpenRouter) is a one-env-var switch; surfcall is the sole integration point with the external API.
notable_patterns: Manual agentic tool-use loop bounded by max_iters to prevent infinite loops
Prompt injection defense: API results treated as data not instructions, explicitly stated in system prompt
Allow-list of tool names enforced in surfcall_tools.call() — agent cannot call write endpoints even if injected
Per-user sliding-window rate limiter protecting ~90 req/min upstream budget
Message chunking for Telegram's 4096-char limit
Dual LLM provider: Anthropic Haiku for reliability, OpenRouter free model as zero-cost default

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean two-part structure: bot/ (Python agent) and app/+components/ (Next.js landing). Each Python module has a single responsibility and a module-level docstring explaining its role and design decisions.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — function and variable names are descriptive and match their behavior (handle_message, resolve_command, chunk_message, tools_for_llm, etc.).
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually good for a hackathon. Module-level docstrings explain design rationale. Inline comments explain non-obvious decisions (e.g. why BLE001 is suppressed, why the free model is default). README is bilingual and accurate.
code_organization.reasoning: The Python bot code reads like production-quality library code: injected dependencies, no global mutable state except the in-memory rate limiter, explicit degradation paths. The .next build directory is mistakenly committed, and no tests exist despite the code being architected for testability.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Secrets come only from environment variables. config.py validates required vars at startup and raises a message naming the missing var name, never a value. No hardcoded tokens or keys found anywhere.
production_readiness.security_notes: Prompt injection defense is explicitly encoded in the system prompt and in surfcall_tools (API results treated as data). Tool allow-list prevents write operations. Privacy: no personal data stored, API already masks cedulas/coords. Missing: no Dockerfile, no process manager config, no webhook mode (runs long-polling only).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single-process long-polling bot with in-memory rate limiter — not horizontally scalable. Rate limiter resets on restart. For a crisis scenario with high traffic this is a real bottleneck. Landing page on Vercel scales fine.
production_readiness.reasoning: Solid fundamentals (error handling, secrets, rate limiting, graceful degradation) but no CI, no tests, no logging, no deployment config, and in-memory-only state. The surfcall dependency is a GitHub-only package not on PyPI, making reproducible deploys fragile.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, functional implementation — there is a live bot at t.me/DEV_VEZbot per README, a demo GIF committed, and actual OpenAPI spec authored against the live SOS Venezuela API. Not boilerplate. However, created and fully built within a single day (all 13 commits on June 29, 2026).
maturity.reasoning: No test files exist anywhere despite the code being architected for offline testability (injected dependencies, pure functions). No CI pipeline. The critical surfcall dependency is a non-PyPI GitHub repo that could vanish or break. The project is a working hackathon prototype, not a maintained service.

viability.reasoning: Strong concept and execution for a hackathon: Telegram reaches Venezuelans without app installs, Spanish-first design is appropriate, natural-language interface lowers the barrier for non-technical users in crisis. Real data integration with the SOS Venezuela 2026 API is demonstrated. Key risks: the surfcall library is the team's own unpublished package (single point of failure), long-polling won't scale under crisis-level load, and there is no persistence so the bot state resets on any restart. If the surfcall dependency were stabilized and the bot moved to webhook mode with a process manager, this would be genuinely deployable.

key_features: Natural-language Telegram interface in Spanish (and English) for querying SOS Venezuela 2026 humanitarian data
AI agent loop using Claude Haiku 4.5 or free Llama 3.3 70B via OpenRouter
People search: query missing/found persons by name via /buscar or free text
Map reports: collapsed buildings, gas leaks, blocked roads, shelters, supply centers
Aggregate statistics on missing/found counts
Latest verified earthquake news via Telegram
Per-user sliding-window rate limiting (8 req/min default)
Prompt-injection defense: API results treated as data, not instructions
Tool allow-list: only 5 public read endpoints exposed to the LLM
Offline/recorded mode for $0 testing without network
Next.js 16 landing page deployed on Vercel with ES/EN i18n
MCP server mode to expose the SOS Venezuela API to Claude Code/Cursor
analysis.merge_potential: Low-medium. The bot is a self-contained Telegram client over a specific external API and has no shared database or auth layer to merge with. It could complement a shelter-mapping or people-finder project by providing a conversational Telegram interface to that project's API, if the target project exposes an OpenAPI spec (which surfcall can consume). The landing page is redundant with a full-stack project.
red_flags: Critical dependency on surfcall (git+https://github.com/GeckoVision/surfcall) — unpublished, not on PyPI, single point of failure for the entire bot
No test files exist despite the code being explicitly architected for offline testability
No CI pipeline — no automated quality gate
In-memory rate limiter resets on every restart; under crisis load, restarts lose all state
Long-polling mode only — not horizontally scalable, one process handles all traffic
No logging — failed tool calls and agent errors are silently degraded with no observability
.next build artifacts committed to the repository (should be gitignored)
No Dockerfile or process manager config — deployment is manual and undocumented beyond README

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake victims in Venezuela need immediate access to shelter locations, missing-persons data, water safety information, and damage reports. The project gives them all of this through Telegram — a platform already on their phones — in plain Spanish, with no app install, no account, and no cost. The integration is against the real SOS Venezuela 2026 API (not mocked data), and the natural-language interface removes the technical barrier for non-developers in crisis. The choice of Telegram as the channel is deliberate and well-reasoned for Venezuelan connectivity conditions.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The Vercel landing page at https://ayuda-venezuela-wine.vercel.app/ loads correctly and presents a polished, bilingual (ES/EN) product page with a direct "Abrir en Telegram" CTA pointing to t.me/DEV_VEZbot. The landing page itself is real and functional. The actual Telegram bot's live status cannot be verified via WebFetch — it requires opening Telegram, so the end-to-end product is "partial" rather than fully confirmed. The repo README and a demo GIF committed to the repo both indicate the bot was running at hackathon submission time.

diffusion.recommended_angle: The free Telegram bot that lets earthquake victims in Venezuela ask for help in plain Spanish — find shelters, search for missing people, check water safety — no app, no signup, works on any phone with Telegram.
gaps: No demo video showing the bot in action (a 30-second screen recording of a real conversation would dramatically improve shareability)
No screenshots of actual bot responses in the landing page or repo
No evidence of real users yet (0 stars, no testimonials)
The Telegram bot may not be running 24/7 since it is a single-process long-polling script with no hosting config — if it is down, the CTA leads nowhere

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Ask @DEV_VEZbot in plain Spanish where to find a shelter, safe water, or a missing loved one — free, no app, no signup, powered by live SOS Venezuela data on Telegram.

name: AyudaVE — Red P2P de Coordinación Humanitaria
db_description:
¿Qué construimos?

Construimos AyudaVE, una red P2P de coordinación humanitaria para Venezuela que conecta personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, dinero, cripto, transporte o tiempo con centros de acopio verificados que realmente lo necesitan.

AyudaVE permite que cualquier persona pueda ayudar en menos de un minuto, sin registro obligatorio para donar, buscar centros o reportar información. Los centros de acopio pueden administrar sus necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, solicitudes de voluntarios/transporte y actualizaciones.

La idea central es simple:

No se trata solo de donar. Se trata de saber qué falta, dónde falta, quién lo necesita, quién lo dona, quién lo transporta y si realmente llegó.

¿Qué problema resuelve?

Después de una emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria suele quedar dispersa en WhatsApp, redes sociales, formularios, grupos y listas manuales. Esto genera varios problemas:

* Duplicación de esfuerzos.
* Centros saturados con ciertos insumos.
* Falta de visibilidad sobre necesidades reales.
* Información desactualizada.
* Riesgo de fraude.
* Poca claridad sobre si la ayuda realmente llegó.
* Personas queriendo ayudar, pero sin saber a dónde ir o qué llevar.

AyudaVE organiza esa coordinación en una plataforma simple, rápida y mobile-first, enfocada en conectar ayuda real con necesidades reales.

¿Para quién es?

AyudaVE está pensada para:

* Personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, insumos, dinero, cripto o transporte.
* Voluntarios que quieren saber dónde ayudar.
* Transportistas que pueden mover ayuda entre zonas.
* Centros de acopio que necesitan administrar inventario, necesidades y horarios.
* Verificadores que pueden confirmar qué centros están activos.
* Comunidades, iglesias, colegios, ONGs, instituciones y grupos ciudadanos organizando ayuda en campo.

¿Cómo funciona?

La plataforma tiene varios flujos principales:

1. Quiero ayudar
    La persona indica su ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puede aportar. La app le muestra centros cercanos y necesidades urgentes.
2. Quiero donar
    El usuario puede donar insumos físicos, comida, agua, medicinas, Pago Móvil, transferencia o cripto. También puede reportar su donación con evidencia.
3. Quiero ser voluntario
    Los voluntarios pueden registrarse, indicar sus habilidades y apoyar con transporte, clasificación, verificación, recolección o difusión.
4. Soy un centro de acopio
    Los centros pueden administrar stock, necesidades urgentes, métodos de donación, horarios, actualizaciones, solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
5. Mapa y necesidades urgentes
    Los usuarios pueden explorar centros cercanos, filtrar por estado/municipio/parroquia y ver qué se necesita con mayor urgencia.
6. Trazabilidad de donaciones
    La meta es que cada donación pueda pasar por estados como: reportada, pendiente de confirmar, confirmada por el centro, usada/entregada y evidenciada.

¿Qué deberían probar primero?

1. Entrar a:
    https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/
2. Tocar “Quiero ayudar” o “Quiero donar”.
3. Elegir una ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puedes aportar.
4. Revisar centros de acopio y necesidades urgentes.
5. Probar el flujo de registrar una donación o crear un centro de acopio.
6. Desde el rol de centro, probar la administración de necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, voluntarios y transporte.

Diferencial

AyudaVE no busca ser solo otro mapa de centros de acopio.

La visión es convertirla en una capa operativa P2P que conecte donantes, voluntarios, transportistas y centros verificados usando:

* Inventario en tiempo real.
* Necesidades urgentes.
* Centros verificados.
* Métodos de donación.
* Solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
* Trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Evidencia de recepción y entrega.

Integración con otros proyectos del hackathon

Queremos integrarnos con una API centralizada del hackathon para consumir y compartir datos de centros, necesidades, stock, donaciones, voluntarios y reportes.

AyudaVE puede funcionar como la interfaz P2P operativa para que la data de otros proyectos se convierta en acción:

qué llevar, a dónde llevarlo, quién lo recibe y si fue confirmado.

Qué necesita el equipo

Necesitamos data real y actualizada de centros de acopio: ubicación, responsables, horarios, contactos, métodos de donación, inventario disponible, necesidades urgentes y capacidad de recepción.

También necesitamos voluntarios/verificadores en campo para confirmar qué centros están activos, qué insumos realmente necesitan y qué ayuda ya llegó.

Además, buscamos apoyo para:

* Validación con centros de acopio reales.
* Integración con APIs centralizadas del hackathon.
* Mejoras UX/UI mobile-first.
* Integración con WhatsApp.
* Automatización de inventario y stock mínimo.
* Sistema de trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Difusión con comunidades, aliados e instituciones.

Estado actual

AyudaVE ya está operativa en:

https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/

La plataforma cuenta con flujos para donantes, voluntarios, centros de acopio, mapa, necesidades, métodos de pago, inventario, actualizaciones y administración/verificación.

analysis.summary: AyudaVE is a real, actively deployed humanitarian platform (ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com) for Venezuela earthquake response. It is a Node.js server (no framework, native http module) with a hand-rolled router, dual-engine DB layer (SQLite for dev, PostgreSQL for prod), and a vanilla JS SPA frontend. The single server.js is 1082 lines of genuine, dense production logic — not boilerplate — covering missing persons, aid centers, donations, volunteers, emergency directory, seismic data, hospital OCR search, AI-powered photo matching (Gemini), and more. The project integrates 8+ external data sources and has real deployment infrastructure (Docker + Cloud Run, VM + Caddy + systemd, backup scripts). Significant gaps: no tests, no CI/CD pipeline, and in-memory rate limiting/session state that breaks under multi-instance Cloud Run scaling.
analysis.project_type: Humanitarian coordination web platform (Node.js + vanilla JS SPA)

database: PostgreSQL via pg driver (production, Cloud SQL)

architecture.pattern: Monolith — single-file server with hand-rolled REST router + static file serving
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw SQL via thin abstraction layer (store.js) that switches between SQLite (store-sqlite.js) and PostgreSQL (store-pg.js) via DATABASE_URL env var. All queries are parameterized; no ORM used.
architecture.api_design: REST JSON API with ~40 endpoints; route matching via compiled pattern array. Auth via Bearer token in Authorization header (session tokens stored in DB). Some GET endpoints expose CDN cache headers.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Good for a single-file server: DB abstraction in store*.js, storage abstraction in storage.js, text normalization in text-normalize.js, search logic in person-search.js, external imports in import-*.js, AI in assistant-gemini.js.
architecture.modularity: Server logic is monolithic (all API handlers in server.js) but supporting modules are well separated. Each external data source has its own import module.
notable_patterns: Dual-DB strategy: SQLite for local dev, PG for prod, switched by env var
Idempotent schema migration on startup with ADD COLUMN try/catch pattern
Privacy-preserving visit counting (SHA-256 IP+UA hash per day, salt not stored)
Gemini AI integration for photo-based missing person search with graceful degradation
Collaborative ingestion bookmarklet to bypass geo-blocking of source data
In-memory rate limiting (RL Map) and brute-force lockout (loginAttempts Map) — not multi-instance safe
scrypt PIN hashing with per-user salt and timing-safe compare

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root with server.js as monolith, import-*.js modules per data source, store*.js for DB engines, public/ for frontend SPA, deploy/ for infra scripts, docs/ with full documentation set.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent: file and function names are consistently descriptive and in Spanish matching the domain, variable names are clear.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Exceptional for a hackathon: CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING.md, DOCUMENTACION.md, DESARROLLO.md, DESPLIEGUE.md, INTEGRACIONES.md, MANUAL-USUARIO.md, architecture SVG. .env.example is thorough with inline comments.
code_organization.reasoning: The module separation is thoughtful and naming is excellent, but cramming all 40+ API handlers into one 1082-line server.js creates a maintainability risk. The documentation quality is far above hackathon norms.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: All secrets via environment variables; .env.example documents every variable with clear [PROD] annotations; .env in .gitignore; no secrets found in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: Strong: scrypt PIN hashing with random salt and timing-safe compare; rate limiting (120 req/min/IP); login brute-force lockout (5 attempts, 10-min ban); CSP, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options headers; path traversal protection on static serving; safeUrl() to block XSS via javascript: URIs; CORS restricted to ingest endpoint only.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: In-memory rate limiting (RL Map) and brute-force lockout (loginAttempts Map) are instance-local and will not work correctly when Cloud Run scales to multiple instances. PostgreSQL pool is configured (PG_POOL env). GCS for shared image storage is correct. Seed/init locks use DB metrics table to prevent multi-instance races.
production_readiness.reasoning: Deployed in production with real traffic, proper security headers, scrypt auth, env-based config, Docker + Cloud Run + systemd deployment paths, backup scripts, and a /healthz endpoint. Main gaps are no tests, no CI, and in-memory state that breaks multi-instance scaling.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — actively deployed at ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com with real data (missing persons, aid centers). Code is dense, domain-specific logic with no AI-generated skeleton patterns.
maturity.reasoning: The project is live and functional with a CHANGELOG showing two documentation versions and active commits through June 29. However, only 5 commits, 2 contributors, no automated tests, and no CI/CD pipeline limit the maturity score despite the code quality.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed to a real domain with real earthquake victims data integrated from 8+ Venezuelan sources. The dual-DB strategy, Dockerfile, systemd service, Caddy config, and backup scripts mean it can be spun up on any cloud VM in minutes. The lack of tests is a risk for safe ongoing contributions but does not block deployment.

key_features: Missing/found persons registry with fuzzy name search (accent/typo tolerant) and AI photo matching via Gemini 2.5 Flash
Aid center (centro de acopio) management with inventory tracking (entradas/salidas/beneficiarios) and logistics dashboard
Donation tracking with automatic inventory entry creation for physical goods
Volunteer registration and application to aid centers
Emergency directory (hospitals, ambulances, fire departments) auto-imported from redayudavenezuela.com
Hospital patient search from OCR dataset
Seismic data (USGS aftershock feed) refreshed every 15 minutes
Service status reports (electricity/water/medicine/fuel) from reporte-ve API
Affected buildings data from terremotovenezuela.com
Help requests (SOS) module with urgency levels
Lost/found pets module
Admin dashboard with time-series charts, live activity feed, and logistics overview
Collaborative bookmarklet ingestion to bypass geo-blocking of Venezuelan data sources
Privacy-preserving anonymous visitor metrics (IP hashed, not stored)
analysis.merge_potential: High — already deployed to production at ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com with real Venezuelan earthquake data. Covers the broadest set of humanitarian domains of any single project reviewed. The platform is functional end-to-end and the clean env-var configuration makes it deployable immediately on any cloud provider.
red_flags: No automated tests of any kind — entire codebase is untested
No CI/CD pipeline — no .github/actions or equivalent
In-memory rate limiting (RL Map) and brute-force lockout (loginAttempts Map) will not function correctly when Cloud Run scales beyond one instance, creating a security gap under load
All 40+ API handlers crammed into a single 1082-line server.js — maintainability risk for new contributors
SQL queries stored as ad-hoc strings throughout server.js (though all appear parameterized, making injection unlikely but hard to audit at scale)
Only 5 commits total — difficult to assess iteration history or contribution patterns

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake humanitarian coordination in Venezuela is genuinely fragmented across WhatsApp groups, social media, manual spreadsheets, and individual phone calls. AyudaVE directly tackles this coordination gap with a unified platform covering: missing/found persons with AI photo matching, aid center inventory management, donation tracking with traceability, volunteer/transporter routing, emergency directory, seismic aftershock data, hospital patient search, SOS requests, and lost pets. Each of these is a real, documented pain point that aid workers and victims face in the first 72-96 hours after a major earthquake. The platform integrates 8+ real Venezuelan data sources (USGS, redayudavenezuela.com, terremotovenezuela.com, reporte-ve), showing genuine domain research rather than speculative feature lists.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch returned HTTP 403 Forbidden for both https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/ and https://ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/ — this is consistent with Cloudflare bot-protection blocking headless scrapers rather than the site being truly down. The technical analysis recorded signals.accessible=true during code review, and the team explicitly states the platform is operational with real data. A demo video exists at https://youtu.be/nFc4Y9NcahA. Independent verification by a human browser is needed to confirm current status, but the deployment infrastructure (Cloud Run + Caddy + systemd + /healthz endpoint) is production-grade.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Después del terremoto, la ayuda se pierde en grupos de WhatsApp y listas descoordinadas. AyudaVE es la plataforma que organiza todo en un solo lugar: donantes, voluntarios, transportistas y centros de acopio verificados — con inventario en tiempo real, búsqueda de personas desaparecidas con IA, y trazabilidad de cada donación.
gaps: No screenshots in submission materials — social posts need visual assets from the team
Demo video not analyzed (YouTube link only, no thumbnail/preview confirmed)
No confirmed real-users metric — platform is live but user adoption data is unknown
The 403 on WebFetch means automated promotional tools cannot scrape the site for previews; team should ensure Open Graph tags and link previews work correctly

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AyudaVE organiza toda la coordinación humanitaria post-terremoto en un solo lugar: donantes, centros de acopio, voluntarios y búsqueda de desaparecidos con IA — para que la ayuda llegue donde realmente hace falta.

name: Beacon Rescate
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?
"Rescatista": Un sistema de triangulación en tiempo real diseñado para entornos de desastre. Utiliza escaneo de protocolos de red (ARP/Wi-Fi) y métricas de latencia para localizar dispositivos móviles sin necesidad de infraestructura de red previa.

## ¿Para quién es? ¿Cómo ayuda?
Diseñado para equipos de búsqueda y rescate en zonas sin cobertura. Permite localizar víctimas basándose en la señal activa de sus dispositivos (smartphones), proporcionando una ubicación visual en un radar en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?
1. Iniciar el escaneo en el entorno de pruebas.
2. Observar cómo el sistema filtra el "ruido" y unifica la señal del objetivo en el radar.
3. Verificar la persistencia de datos en la base de datos local (SQLite).

analysis.summary: Beacon-rescate is a single-file Python/Kivy Android app (301 LOC) that attempts offline BLE and Wi-Fi proximity scanning to help rescuers detect nearby devices (potential survivors) in disaster zones. The concept — infrastructure-free detection using Bluetooth and local WiFi — is genuinely relevant to earthquake rescue. However the implementation contains critical bugs, uses a Windows-only ping command on an Android target, never invokes the WiFi callback (bypassing the app's own processing pipeline), and has no real triangulation despite claiming it. The README claims Cython/C math acceleration but no such code exists in the repository.
analysis.project_type: Mobile Android app (Python/Kivy compiled to APK)

architecture.pattern: Monolithic single-file application
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw sqlite3 with parameterized queries directly in app and helper class methods
architecture.api_design: None — fully offline local app with no network API
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Minimal: BLE scanner, WiFi monitor, radar widget, and SQLite logic are split into classes but all in one file with tight coupling and cross-class DB access
architecture.modularity: Very low — single main.py file, no modules, no packages
notable_patterns: BLE scanning via Android JNI (jnius/autoclass) for native Bluetooth LE
RSSI-to-distance estimation using log-distance path loss model
Kivy Clock for periodic UI refresh
WAL-mode SQLite for concurrent thread writes
Hash-based angle assignment for radar display (pseudo-random, not real triangulation)

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root: main.py, requirements.txt, README.md, gitignore. No subdirectories, no separation of concerns at file level.
code_organization.naming_quality: Spanish-language identifiers throughout (consistent within the project but non-standard for open-source collaboration); method and class names are descriptive.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers install steps and architecture claims but the claimed Cython/C component does not exist in the repo. Inline comments are helpful but scattered.
code_organization.reasoning: The project is a single-file monolith with no module structure. Code is readable but tightly coupled, and the repository structure offers no room to grow without a major refactor.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets used — fully offline app.
production_readiness.security_notes: No security concerns for secrets, but the WiFi scanner does a blind subnet sweep (192.168.x.1-254) using subprocess shell=True with unvalidated input, which is a code-quality risk.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Fully offline single-device app — scalability is not applicable, but the LIMIT 1 query in cargar_datos_en_mapa means only one device is ever shown on the radar at a time, which is a functional regression.
production_readiness.reasoning: Error handling consists entirely of bare print() statements with no recovery logic. No logging framework, no env/config layer, no deployment manifests (Dockerfile, CI, buildozer.spec). The app has critical bugs that prevent its core WiFi flow from working.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real custom logic (BLE RSSI math, WiFi subnet sweep, Kivy radar widget) but with significant bugs that prevent correct operation.
maturity.reasoning: 4 commits over a single day, 1 contributor, no tests, no CI. The WiFi monitor never calls its callback (data flows through a duplicate DB path instead), the ping command uses Windows syntax on an Android target, and the radar only ever shows the single most recent device due to a SQL LIMIT 1 bug.

viability.reasoning: The core concept — offline BLE/WiFi proximity detection for search and rescue without internet infrastructure — is highly relevant and technically sound in principle. However the current implementation has multiple show-stopping bugs: the WiFi callback is wired but never invoked, the ping command is Windows-only and will fail on Android/Linux, and the radar display is broken (LIMIT 1). The README also falsely claims Cython/C acceleration that does not exist. These are fixable issues but require substantial rework before the app could be tested on a real device.

key_features: Offline BLE Low Energy device scanning using Android native APIs via jnius
WiFi hotspot subnet sweep using ping to detect connected devices
RSSI-to-distance estimation with smoothing filter (5-sample rolling average)
Kivy-based radar widget displaying detected devices at estimated distances
Local SQLite database (WAL mode) as unified data store for BLE and WiFi detections
Toggle between BLE and WiFi scanning modes at runtime
analysis.merge_potential: Low. The app is a standalone Android tool with a unique offline-detection concept, but its bugs, single-file structure, and Kivy framework make integration into a broader web-based relief platform extremely difficult without a full rewrite.
red_flags: WiFi subnet scan uses 'ping -n 1 -w 100' (Windows syntax) — will fail silently on Android/Linux target; correct flags are '-c 1 -W 0.1'
MonitorWifiHotspot.escanear_dispositivos_conectados() never calls self.callback_funcion — it writes directly to DB, completely bypassing procesar_dispositivo_wifi in the app
cargar_datos_en_mapa uses 'LIMIT 1' — radar only ever shows one device regardless of how many are detected
README claims 'Lógica Matemática: C (Vía Cython)' but zero C or Cython files exist anywhere in the repo
Radar angle is computed as hash(mac) % 360 — no real triangulation, positions are pseudo-random and not spatially meaningful
subprocess.run with shell=True and a dynamically constructed IP string, though the IP is locally generated so low practical risk
No tests, no CI, no buildozer.spec — APK cannot be built from this repo as-is

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The core concept — offline BLE/WiFi proximity scanning to locate survivors without any network infrastructure — directly addresses a genuine, critical need in earthquake rescue: finding people buried or trapped in areas where cell towers and internet are destroyed. The idea is technically sound in principle. However, the current implementation does not actually solve the problem because it has multiple show-stopping bugs: the WiFi scanning callback is wired but never invoked, the ping command uses Windows-only syntax that will silently fail on Android/Linux, the radar only ever displays one device (LIMIT 1 SQL bug), and the README falsely claims Cython/C acceleration that does not exist. The app cannot be built from the repo as-is (no buildozer.spec). So the problem is real and severe, but the product does not yet solve it.
