- Handles sensitive data carefully by avoiding unnecessary exposure of names, document IDs, raw messages, tokens, or private payloads in logs and analytics.

## What should people try first?

Start with the Telegram bot:

https://t.me/encontrados_ve_bot

Try:

- `/ayuda` to see how it works.
- `/buscar Nombre Apellido` to search by name.
- `/buscar V12345678` to search by Venezuelan ID.
- `/lista` to browse records.
- `/fuentes` to see current data sources.

The project is open source here:

https://github.com/edwinvrgs/found-people-ve-bot

analysis.summary: A fully operational Telegram bot (live at t.me/encontrados_ve_bot) that aggregates found-people records from 5 Venezuelan earthquake data sources, deduplicates them, stores them in PostgreSQL, and exposes search via Telegram commands and a REST API. Built in TypeScript with Fastify, Prisma ORM, Pino logging, PostHog analytics, and a comprehensive test suite. Committed 77 times in 3 days with 2 contributors — this is genuinely working software serving real users, not boilerplate.
analysis.project_type: Telegram bot + REST API backend for earthquake victim people-finder

architecture.pattern: Layered monolith (config → repositories → services → http/telegram)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Prisma ORM for schema/migrations; raw Prisma.$queryRaw with tagged-template parameterized SQL for bulk inserts, updates, and full-text searches
architecture.api_design: Fastify REST API with two tiers: public paginated endpoints and authenticated admin/external-report endpoints; Telegram webhook endpoint
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Well-separated: config/env, repositories (DB access), services (business logic), http (routes/security/schemas), telegram (bot commands), ingestion (source adapters + dedupe)
architecture.modularity: Distinct modules per concern; ingestion layer is pluggable via source adapter pattern with 5 real source integrations
notable_patterns: Multi-source ingestion pipeline with per-page signature deduplication to detect stale pagination
IngestMatchCache for in-memory lookup during bulk upsert (source_hash, document_id, source_url)
Levenshtein-based fuzzy name similarity for duplicate audit and merge planning

Timing-safe token comparison (node:crypto timingSafeEqual) for external API auth

Missing Dockerfile/deployment manifest is the main gap.

No Dockerfile is the main maturity gap.

viability.reasoning: This is already deployed and in active use during the earthquake emergency. The data ingestion pipeline pulls from 5 real Venezuelan earthquake databases. The Telegram bot provides search and browse functionality to people looking for survivors. External API lets other services integrate. The code quality is among the highest in the cohort — this is a serious production system built rapidly under pressure.

key_features: Multi-source ingestion from 5 Venezuelan earthquake people-finder APIs (desaparecidos-terremoto, encuentralos, desaparecidosvenezuela, venezuelatebusca, sosvenezuela2026)
Automated deduplication with Levenshtein fuzzy name matching, document-ID merging, and audit trail in FoundPeopleMergeAudit table
Telegram bot with /buscar, /lista, /fuentes, /sugerencia commands and inline keyboard pagination

External report submission endpoint with idempotency key support (allows other services to report found people)
Admin Telegram commands (/admin_stats, /admin_delete) gated by chat ID
Per-surface rate limiting (public API, Telegram chat, external reports, admin)
PostHog analytics with hashed/salted user identifiers for privacy-safe usage tracking
analysis.merge_potential: High. This project is already live and functional, with an external REST API that other projects can consume directly. The ingestion layer (5 sources, deduplication, fuzzy matching) is the most sophisticated people-finder data pipeline in the cohort and could serve as the authoritative backend for a broader family-reunification platform. Requires adding a Dockerfile and Redis-backed rate limiting for multi-instance deployment.
red_flags: No Dockerfile or deployment manifest — deployment process is undocumented
In-memory rate limiting and session state (pending actions, search tokens) are lost on process restart and cannot scale horizontally
guardAdminRequest uses non-timing-safe string comparison for INGEST_SECRET (inconsistent with guardExternalReportRequest which correctly uses timingSafeEqual)
No startup validation of required environment variables — missing DATABASE_URL or TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN would only error on first use
Ingestion is triggered manually (npm run ingest:found-people) with no built-in scheduler — data freshness depends on external cron job setup

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After an earthquake, found-person records get scattered across spreadsheets, social posts, and disconnected websites run by different community groups. Families searching for survivors waste critical hours checking each source manually. This bot ingests 5 real Venezuelan earthquake people-finder databases, deduplicates them with Levenshtein fuzzy matching, and makes the unified dataset searchable via Telegram by name or ID number — exactly where Venezuelan families already communicate. The problem is acute, the solution is targeted, and the bot is live during the active emergency.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The Telegram bot is live at t.me/encontrados_ve_bot and accepts /buscar, /lista, /fuentes commands right now. There is no public web UI or video demo. Anyone with Telegram can test it immediately, but it cannot be demonstrated in a browser screenshot or embedded in a social post without a screen recording.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the human story: 'One search on Telegram. Five databases. Every found person in Venezuela.' Show the bot commands (/buscar V12345678) and the five integrated sources by name. Invite families to try it and invite other developers to use the REST API to integrate. A 30-second screen recording of a live Telegram search would massively amplify this — the team should record one immediately.
gaps: no demo video or screen recording
no screenshots of the bot in action
no web landing page or visual branding to embed in posts
no real-user testimonials yet

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Encuentra a tus familiares con un solo comando: el bot de Telegram que unifica todos los registros de personas encontradas tras el terremoto en Venezuela.

name: GuacaMalla - Mesh sync entre dispositivos
db_description:
GuacaMalla es una red de comunicación de emergencia descentralizada y Offline-First, diseñada para situaciones de catástrofe (terremotos, cortes masivos, colapso de infraestructura) donde internet y las torres celulares dejan de funcionar.

## ¿Cuál es la meta?

Nuestra meta es salvar vidas asegurando que ninguna persona herida o aislada se quede incomunicada en momentos críticos.

## ¿Cómo funciona?

La app convierte teléfonos Android en balizas de radio independientes que emiten frames de 119 bytes firmados con Ed25519 vía Bluetooth (BLE 5). Los mensajes SOS y coordenadas GPS saltan de dispositivo en dispositivo formando una malla descentralizada sin necesidad de servidores ni internet. Cuando un teléfono alcanza una zona con señal actúa como "mula de datos" y sube los frames acumulados al backend opcional, que los re-verifica criptográficamente antes de persistirlos.

El enlace es intencionalmente abierto — un SOS es público, cualquiera en alcance debe recibirlo y retransmitirlo. La confianza vive en el payload: cada frame va firmado con Ed25519 y cualquier manipulación rompe la firma, de modo que el siguiente salto lo descarta silenciosamente.

**El backend es opcional.** La malla funciona 100% offline y sin infraestructura.

## Equipo

- Rodrigo Rivas — Product + UI/UX + Frontend · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rodrigorivasco)
- Victor Maldonado — Fullstack + Mobile Native · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/vmlcode/)
- David Gonzalez — Backend + Mobile Native + QA · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/david-gonzalez-dev2012/)
- Jaime Stalislav — Backend + Mobile Native + QA
- Carlos Ramirez — PM + Plataforma · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/carlosjramirez/)
- Cristopher Avila — Frontend · [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/cristopher-adrián-ávila-lópez-937994402)

## Arquitectura

| Plano | Radio | Payload | Estándar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control / SOS | BLE GAP Broadcaster/Observer | 22 B payload + 32 B pubkey + 64 B firma | BLE 5 Extended Advertising |
| Datos (ligero) | Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) | ≤ 255 B | NAN Service Discovery |
| Datos (pesado) | Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) | > 255 B | NAN Data Path (NDP), efímero |

El frame de 22 bytes incluye lat/lon (int32 E7), timestamp Unix y tipo de mensaje. La autenticidad se verifica con Ed25519 en cada salto — el nodo receptor nunca confía en el remitente.

analysis.summary: GuacaMalla Net is a serious, production-oriented disaster-response mesh network system built for offline-first earthquake response. It consists of an Android Kotlin app implementing BLE flood-routing with Ed25519-signed 22-byte payloads, and a Bun/Fastify TypeScript backend that aggregates messages, provides WebSocket real-time push, and handles a cryptographically-verified M-of-N rescue confirmation ("resolve") flow. The system is designed to operate entirely without internet infrastructure — phones form a BLE mesh, store-and-forward SOS frames, and batch-upload to the backend when connectivity returns. The code is genuinely sophisticated: custom leaky-bucket rate limiting keyed on device IDs, timing-safe API key comparison, geo-radius validation for witness claims, TTL-bounded flood routing, BouncyCastle Ed25519 signing on Android, in-memory Supabase fallback for dev, and a full Supabase schema with RLS. This is not boilerplate — it is an original, domain-specific implementation with real algorithmic depth.
analysis.project_type: Android app + TypeScript backend — offline-first BLE mesh SOS network with cloud aggregation layer

architecture.pattern: Offline-first BLE mesh (Android) + REST/WebSocket aggregation backend (Bun/Fastify) + Supabase persistence
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (BaaS) with raw SQL schema; Room DAO on Android for local SQLite persistence
architecture.api_design: REST endpoints for ingest (POST /ingest), locations (GET/POST), channels, resolve flow (POST /resolve, POST /resolve/evidence), plus WebSocket /ws with channel subscription model
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Backend is cleanly split into db/, mesh/, crypto/, security/, channels/, locations/, resolve/, ws/, dashboard/ modules; Android splits into ble/, mesh/, crypto/, ingest/, backend/, aware/, service/, ui/, proto/ packages
architecture.modularity: Monorepo with packages/shared (TypeScript types + crypto shared between backend and potential web dashboard), backend/, android/; workspace dependencies via Bun workspaces
notable_patterns: Ed25519 cryptographic signing on every BLE frame and official record
Store-and-forward mesh routing: phones relay other nodes' SOS frames from local SQLite after originator goes offline
Data-mule upload pattern: WorkManager batches local SQLite frames and uploads when connectivity returns
M-of-N witness quorum (configurable) for rescue confirmation with cooldown sweep
Custom leaky-bucket rate limiter keyed on Ed25519 device ID, not IP
Dual transport: BLE advertising + Wi-Fi Aware (NaN) — NanMessenger is implemented but not yet wired into the foreground service
In-memory fallback for all repos when Supabase is unconfigured, enabling `bun run dev` with zero setup

code_organization.directory_structure: Very clean: backend/src/{mesh,crypto,security,channels,locations,resolve,ws,dashboard,db}/, android split into granular packages, packages/shared for cross-platform types. CLAUDE.md and DESIGN.md at root and in each major subdirectory.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent: names like FloodRouter, DedupeCache, LeakyBucket, IngestUploadWorker, OfficialRecordVerifier, verifyWitnessSignature, haversineKm are precise and self-documenting
code_organization.documentation_quality: High: extensive inline comments explaining security rationale, docs/ folder with protocol-flows.md, payload-binary-layout.md, crypto.md, architecture docs in Spanish and English, Postman collection, backend_final.md
code_organization.reasoning: 96 commits from 5 contributors with deliberate architecture documentation at every level. The CLAUDE.md and .cursor/rules files indicate active AI-assisted development but the underlying design decisions are clearly human-driven and domain-expert.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: API keys via environment variables; server refuses to boot without GUACAMAYA_ADMIN_KEY in production (throws at startup); Supabase service-role key never shipped to clients; timing-safe key comparison via timingSafeEqual
production_readiness.security_notes: Ed25519 signatures verified server-side on every ingested frame; geo-radius check on resolve witnesses; per-device-ID leaky bucket (not IP-based); helmet + CORS configured; RLS enabled on all Supabase tables with no anon policies; WebSocket token via sec-websocket-protocol header or query param
production_readiness.scalability_notes: WebSocket client set and rate-limiter LeakyBucket are in-memory — no horizontal scaling without sticky sessions or externalizing state to Redis. Single Bun process. Suitable for hackathon/early deployment scale but would need clustering work for city-scale load.
production_readiness.reasoning: Backend has graceful SIGTERM/SIGINT shutdown, health endpoint, Railway restart policy, Dockerfile, structured Fastify logging, and comprehensive rate limiting. Primary gap is in-memory WS/rate-limit state preventing horizontal scale-out.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real implementation with genuine algorithmic depth — custom BLE flood router, binary payload protocol, cryptographic verification cascade, M-of-N quorum logic with cooldown sweep. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 96 commits, 5 contributors, 17 test files spanning Kotlin unit tests (SignerTest, AgePolicyTest, NodeCatalogTest, PayloadTest, IngestFrameTest, etc.) and TypeScript tests (frame decode, shared types, resolve logic). CI builds and distributes Android APK via Firebase. The only gap is no backend route-level integration tests.

viability.reasoning: This project uniquely solves the hardest constraint of earthquake response in Venezuela: the near-total collapse of cellular and internet infrastructure. The BLE mesh + store-and-forward design means phones that have never connected to the internet can still relay SOS signals across a city block-by-block, and data-mule upload ensures eventual backend aggregation when any phone regains connectivity. The Ed25519 security model prevents spoofed rescue confirmations. No other reviewed project in this hackathon context addresses infrastructure collapse at the physical layer. The Android app is real, the backend is deployable today on Railway, and the resolve/confirmation flow is a concrete differentiator for rescue coordination.

key_features: BLE flood-routing mesh: Ed25519-signed 22-byte frames, hop-TTL bounded, CRC16 validated
Store-and-forward: offline phones relay SOS frames from local Room DB for up to 72h (help frames) or 5min (presence)
Data-mule upload: WorkManager batch-uploads local SQLite to backend when connectivity returns
M-of-N witness quorum for rescue confirmation with geo-radius verification and cooldown sweep
Wi-Fi Aware (NaN) secondary transport implemented (NanMessenger, NanDataPath)
Official downlink: backend signs alert records with Ed25519 and pushes via WebSocket to subscribed devices
Anti-troll cascade: envelope shape → target existence → recency → rate limit → originator exclusion → geo-radius → signature → upload-token
Supabase persistence with in-memory dev fallback; full schema with RLS on all tables
Railway-ready Docker deployment with health endpoint and restart policy
Firebase App Distribution CI for Android APK builds on every push to main
analysis.merge_potential: High — the backend exposes standard REST+WebSocket APIs that any other hackathon project could consume for real-time SOS locations and official alerts. The shared package (packages/shared) provides TypeScript types for interoperability. The resolve flow API is a natural complement to any people-finder or shelter-mapping project.
red_flags: WebSocket client registry and LeakyBucket rate limiter are in-memory — no horizontal scaling without Redis or sticky sessions
Wi-Fi Aware (NanMessenger/NanDataPath) is implemented but not wired into GuacamayaForegroundService — it is dead code today
No backend route-level integration tests — only mesh frame decode tests and shared-lib unit tests; resolve route logic is untested at the HTTP layer
google-services.json committed to repo (acceptable for Android app config but worth noting)
SMS module is a stub (.gitkeep only) — referenced in docs but not implemented
In-memory store fallback silently loses data on restart if Supabase is misconfigured in production

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Telecommunications infrastructure collapse is the defining constraint of earthquake response in Venezuela. When cell towers and internet go down, first responders cannot locate survivors and isolated victims cannot signal for help. GuacaMalla directly attacks this constraint: phones form a BLE mesh entirely without servers or connectivity, relay signed SOS frames hop-by-hop across a city, and batch-upload to a cloud backend the moment any single phone regains signal. This is not a marginal convenience — it addresses the scenario where people die because no communication is possible. No other project in this hackathon context works at the physical-layer infrastructure gap.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: https://guacamalla.org loads a page showing "SOS G" and "Unpacking..." — appears to be a bundled/loading state that never resolves for a visitor. No working landing page, no download link, no CTAs visible. The real product is an Android APK distributed via Firebase App Distribution (CI on every push to main), so the app itself exists and is buildable, but there is no public-facing entry point a non-developer could use today. A demo video exists at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=d21xoFurccc which is the primary shareable asset.

diffusion.recommended_angle: A phone network that works when ALL networks fail — GuacaMalla turns Android phones into a cryptographically-secure rescue mesh for earthquake survivors, zero internet required. Lead with the technical miracle: 22-byte signed BLE frames hopping block-by-block across a destroyed city, with a quorum rescue-confirmation flow so rescuers know which pings are real.
gaps: live-demo is broken (guacamalla.org shows an unresolved loading state)
no Play Store or public APK download link for non-developers
screenshots of the Android app UI not publicly visible
SMS relay module documented but unimplemented (minor credibility gap if mentioned)
Wi-Fi Aware transport implemented but not wired in — should not be claimed as live feature

evaluation.one_line_pitch: GuacaMalla turns ordinary Android phones into a self-organizing rescue mesh — no internet, no cell towers, no servers needed — so earthquake survivors can signal SOS and be found even when all infrastructure has collapsed.

name: Puente Venezuela
db_description:
Puente Venezuela
A web-based humanitarian coordination platform built for the Build4Venezuela hackathon, following the June 24, 2026, earthquakes in Venezuela.

What it does
Connects those in need of help with those who can offer help in real time:

Interactive map with pins showing needs and offers of help
Quick reports — anyone can report a need or offer help without having to create an account
Filters by category — water, food, shelter, medicine, rescue, volunteers, transportation
Real-time status—pending, in progress, or resolved
Responsive design—works on mobile and desktop.

analysis.summary: Puente Venezuela is a map-based humanitarian coordination PWA that lets earthquake victims and volunteers post geo-tagged needs/offers without registration. Built in a single day (June 28-29, 2026) using Lovable.dev (AI-assisted), it has a real Supabase backend with 8 SQL migrations, Supabase Realtime subscriptions, photo upload with client-side image compression, bilingual (ES/EN) UI, a nearby-match algorithm (Haversine), WhatsApp sharing, and a volunteer assignment flow. The feature set is genuinely useful for a disaster scenario. However there are no tests, no CI/CD, no deploy config, the .env file with real credentials is committed, and the entire reports table is open to anonymous writes with no rate limiting — creating serious spam/vandalism risk for a real deployment.
analysis.project_type: Humanitarian coordination PWA (geo-tagged needs/offers map)

infra_deploy: None configured — no Dockerfile, no GitHub Actions, no Vercel/Railway config

architecture.pattern: Full-stack SPA/SSR hybrid (TanStack Start) + Supabase BaaS
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw SQL migrations via Supabase CLI; Supabase JS client (supabase-js v2) used directly as the data-access layer — no ORM
architecture.api_design: TanStack Start createServerFn for server-side mutations (listReports, createReport, updateStatus, assignReport); client-side Supabase JS for storage and realtime subscriptions
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Routes own page logic; reusable components in src/components; server functions isolated in src/lib/*.functions.ts; i18n, upload, distance, PWA in separate lib modules
architecture.modularity: Good for a hackathon: components, lib utilities, and routes are cleanly separated; the UI component library (40+ shadcn-ui primitives) is boilerplate but not in the way
notable_patterns: Zod input validation on every server function
Supabase Realtime subscriptions invalidate TanStack Query cache on table changes
Client-side Haversine distance for nearby-match suggestions
Canvas-based image compression before upload (max 1600px, 0.82 JPEG quality)
Bilingual dictionary-based i18n (ES/EN) stored in React context
PWA service worker via Workbox with strict guards for preview environments
WhatsApp deep-link share from report detail panel

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear: src/routes (pages), src/components (UI), src/lib (utilities and server functions), src/integrations/supabase (client + types), supabase/migrations. No deeply nested confusion.
code_organization.naming_quality: Good: file names match their purpose (reports.functions.ts, attachments.functions.ts, distance.ts, upload.ts). Component names are descriptive.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README exists but is minimal. AGENTS.md present (intent layer). Inline comments are sparse but the code is readable. No JSDoc.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is clean and well-structured for a Lovable.dev-generated project built in under 24 hours. The separation of server functions from UI components is commendable, but there is zero test coverage and no inline documentation beyond occasional comments.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: CRITICAL: .env file with real Supabase project ID, anon key, and URL is committed to the repository. The anon key is designed to be public, but committing .env is still bad practice and the service_role key could end up there too.
production_readiness.security_notes: Anonymous UPDATE on the entire reports table is explicitly granted (USING(true) WITH CHECK(true)) to handle disaster-scenario no-login usage — meaning anyone can mark any report resolved or assign themselves to it. Anonymous INSERT on report_attachments is also fully open with no rate limiting or file-type enforcement beyond the client-side check. No CSRF mitigation beyond TanStack Start's built-in protections.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase scales horizontally; the stateless server functions are fine. However, no pagination on listReports (fetches all rows on every load), no search index on reports, and no rate limiting — a coordinated spam attack could flood the map and render it unusable.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app has a real working backend and proper Supabase RLS, but the deliberate choice to allow fully anonymous writes with no rate limiting is a significant production risk. There is no deploy config, no CI, and credentials are committed. It is functional for a demo but needs hardening before real disaster deployment.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real logic — not empty scaffolding. The reports CRUD, photo upload with compression, real-time subscriptions, Haversine matching, bilingual i18n, and volunteer assignment flow are all genuinely implemented and connected to a live Supabase instance.
maturity.reasoning: Created in a single day via Lovable.dev with 62 commits. Core features work end-to-end but there are zero tests, no CI/CD pipeline, and no deployment configuration. The 8 SQL migrations show iterative feature development but the overall artifact is a functional hackathon prototype, not a mature product.

viability.reasoning: The concept is directly relevant (needs/offers coordination on a live map after earthquakes) and the implementation covers the most critical flows: reporting without signup, map visualization, volunteer assignment, nearby matching, and emergency contact numbers. The blocking risks for real deployment are: fully open anonymous writes (spam), no rate limiting, no moderation, and no deploy infra. These are fixable in days, not weeks, making it viable with effort.

key_features: Anonymous geo-tagged need/offer reports (no login required)
Interactive Leaflet map with urgency-sized, color-coded markers (red=need, green=offer)
Supabase Realtime live updates — map refreshes when any report changes
Nearby-match algorithm: shows opposite-type reports in the same category within an adjustable radius (Haversine)
Volunteer self-assignment with name and contact (marks report in_progress)
Photo upload with client-side canvas compression (max 5 photos, 5MB each, 1600px max)
Bilingual UI (Spanish/English) with localStorage persistence
Advanced filters: type, category, status, time period, min urgency, photos-only
WhatsApp deep-link sharing of individual reports
Emergency contacts page (Venezuela 911, Bomberos 171, Protección Civil, Cruz Roja, FUNVISIS)
PWA with service worker (Workbox) for installability and offline indicator
analysis.merge_potential: Medium. The map+report core is solid and unique enough to stand alone, but the fully open anonymous write model and absence of any spam protection would need resolution before deploying alongside other systems. The bilingual support and WhatsApp integration are directly reusable.
red_flags: .env with real Supabase credentials committed to the repository
Fully open anonymous UPDATE on all reports (anyone can resolve/reassign any report)
No rate limiting on report creation or file uploads — trivially spammable
No tests of any kind (unit, integration, or e2e)
No CI/CD pipeline or deployment configuration
listReports fetches all rows with no pagination — will degrade at scale
Built entirely with Lovable.dev AI generation in under 24 hours — limited human review of edge cases
No content moderation or report flagging mechanism

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake Venezuela has a genuine coordination gap: victims and volunteers scatter information across WhatsApp groups, social media, and word-of-mouth, making it nearly impossible to match needs with available resources. Puente Venezuela directly addresses this by providing a single, geo-tagged, real-time map where anyone can post a need or an offer without creating an account. The category set (water, food, shelter, medicine, rescue, transportation) maps directly to what earthquake victims require in the first 72 hours. The nearby-match algorithm and volunteer assignment flow are genuine operational features, not cosmetic. The WhatsApp integration acknowledges the real communication habits of Venezuelans.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads cleanly at puentevenezuela.lovable.app with a proper bilingual landing page explaining the platform's purpose and user personas (affected people, volunteers, NGOs/authorities). The value proposition is clearly communicated. All statistics counters show zero — no real reports have been submitted yet, so the map is empty. The app appears functionally deployed (Supabase backend is live), but it has no real-world data, making it look inactive to a first-time visitor. Navigation and the overall shell appear functional.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Mapa en tiempo real que conecta víctimas del terremoto en Venezuela con voluntarios al instante — sin registro, funciona en cualquier celular. Built in under 24 hours at Build4Venezuela.
gaps: no demo video showing the map and report flow in action
zero real user data — live counters all show 0, undermining credibility
no screenshots of the map with actual pins
no testimonials or pilot usage
critical security gaps (open anonymous writes, no rate limiting) that could be weaponized if the platform goes viral

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Puente Venezuela: mapa en tiempo real que conecta a víctimas del terremoto con voluntarios sin necesidad de registro — construido en menos de 24 horas para salvar vidas.

name: LocalizadosVzlaBot — Localizador de Personas, Refugios y Donativos Terremoto Venezuela (2026)
db_description:
# Bot de WhatsApp de Ayuda Sismo — Venezuela 2026

Un chatbot de WhatsApp ligero, seguro y optimizado para producción, diseñado para coordinar la asistencia humanitaria y la localización de personas tras el terremoto de Venezuela de 2026. 

El bot consume directamente las APIs públicas de **Localizados Venezuela**, **SOS Venezuela** y **AcopioVE** en tiempo real, ofreciendo información consolidada de forma instantánea sin requerir bases de datos locales ni almacenamiento de estado sensible en el chat.

---
## Características Clave

*   **Búsqueda Dual y Fusión Inteligente (Smart Fusion)**:
    *   Al buscar una persona por nombre o cédula, el bot consulta de forma paralela (mediante `Promise.allSettled` tolerante a fallos) la API oficial de **Localizados Venezuela** y la API comunitaria de **SOS Venezuela**.
    *   **Algoritmo de De-duplicación**: Unifica las respuestas duplicadas utilizando coincidencia exacta sobre el nombre normalizado (removiendo mayúsculas, acentos y espacios innecesarios).
    *   **Priorización de Estatus**: Otorga prioridad absoluta al estado de **Localizado (Confirmado)** si una persona figura en un centro médico o albergue oficial, enriqueciendo la ficha con reportes comunitarios de búsqueda previa de la red SOS, observaciones clínicas y enlaces de consulta de ambas plataformas.
*   **Integración de APIs en Tiempo Real**:
    *   **Localizados Venezuela**: Consulta de personas localizadas en centros de salud y refugios autorizados.
    *   **SOS Venezuela**: Registro comunitario de personas desaparecidas o reportadas a salvo por familiares.
    *   **AcopioVE (Refugios y Centros de Donación)**: Ubica albergues activos y puntos de donación por ciudad, mostrando dirección, estado de ocupación (abierto/lleno) y necesidades inmediatas.
    *   **ResponseGrid (Necesidades de Ayuda)**: Consulta en tiempo real las solicitudes activas de insumos, recursos y voluntariado validadas por coordinadores de emergencia.
    *   **Directorio de Emergencias**: Acceso directo al listado oficial de números de primera respuesta del país.
*   **Búsqueda Inteligente y Sin Falsos Positivos**:
    *   **Por Cédula de Identidad**: Sanitiza la entrada y realiza búsquedas de dígitos exactos sobre la API.
    *   **Por Nombre (Filtrado Estricto de Telegram)**: Solicita un lote amplio de registros y los filtra localmente en memoria. Exige la coincidencia de **todas** las palabras clave ingresadas (insensible a acentos/mayúsculas), eliminando coincidencias parciales molestas (por ejemplo, buscar "Carlos Perez" no retornará otros "Carlos" que no sean "Perez").
    *   **Sanitización de Comandos**: Ignora preposiciones gramaticales al inicio de los comandos (`refugio en Caracas` o `acopio de Valencia` buscan directamente por la ciudad `"Caracas"` o `"Valencia"`).
*   **Caché en Memoria Local**:
    *   Implementa un sistema interno de caché por TTL (`Map` nativo de alto rendimiento) para almacenar temporalmente los resultados de las APIs externas:
        *   Directorio de Emergencias: 5 minutos.
        *   Búsquedas de Refugios: 2 minutos.
        *   Centros de Acopio: 2 minutos.
        *   Necesidades de Ayuda (ResponseGrid): 2 minutos.
        *   Búsquedas de Personas (Búsqueda Dual): 1 minuto.
    *   Esto acelera los tiempos de respuesta del bot y mitiga completamente el riesgo de bloqueos por **Rate Limiting** (120 req/min de la API externa) al reutilizar datos de consultas frecuentes de diferentes usuarios.

*   **Aprovisionamiento Idempotente y Seguro**:
    *   Al iniciar, comprueba si la sesión del bot ya está configurada en la API de FZap.
    *   Realiza un `GET /webhook` para verificar si la dirección de callback ya existe antes de registrarla, **evitando la duplicación de webhooks** que causa respuestas duplicadas o triplicadas en los chats.
*   **Seguridad contra Condiciones de Carrera**:
    *   Procesa los mensajes entrantes de cada chat secuencialmente mediante una cola de tareas asíncronas (`taskQueue`) por JID (ID de usuario de WhatsApp), evitando colisiones cuando un usuario envía ráfagas rápidas de mensajes.
*   **Imagen Docker Altamente Optimizada**:
    *   Dockerfile basado en imágenes ligeras Alpine.
    *   Eliminadas dependencias de compiladores C++ nativos (`make`, `g++`, `python3`) tras remover librerías pesadas como SQLite local.
    *   El contenedor corre bajo un usuario sin privilegios (`fzap`) para garantizar la seguridad del servidor host.

---

## Configuración de Variables de Entorno

Crea un archivo `.env` en la raíz del proyecto (toma como base `.env.example`). A continuación se detallan los parámetros necesarios:

| Variable | Descripción | Ejemplo / Valor Recomendado |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **`PORT`** | Puerto local en el que se ejecuta el servidor del bot. | `3000` |
| **`FZAP_API_URL`** | URL base de la API de FZap. | `http://fzap_api:8080` o `http://163.176.47.42:8081` |
| **`FZAP_API_KEY`** | Token de autorización de administrador de FZap. | `tu_fzap_api_key` |
| **`FZAP_INSTANCE`** | Nombre de la instancia de WhatsApp asignada al bot. | `bot_instance` |
| **`FZAP_WEBHOOK_URL`** | URL pública o interna del webhook de callback del bot. | `http://whatsapp-bot-sismo.local:3000/webhook/messages` |
| **`FZAP_WEBHOOK_SECRET`** | Clave secreta para firmar/validar la autenticidad del webhook (HMAC SHA-256). | `tu_secreto_de_webhook` |
| **`SITE_URL`** | URL del portal web oficial de búsqueda de personas localizadas. | `https://localizadosvenezuela.com` |

---

## Instalación y Despliegue

### Paso 1: Configurar el Entorno
1. Copia la plantilla de variables:
   ```bash
   cp .env.example .env
   ```
2. Completa los valores en el archivo `.env` de acuerdo a tu servidor.

### Paso 2: Compilar e Iniciar los Contenedores
Inicia el entorno usando Docker Compose:
```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose-prod.yml up -d --build
```
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Si estás redesplegando cambios de código del bot en un servidor que ya tiene la sesión de WhatsApp vinculada en el contenedor `fzap_api`, **no apagues ni reinicies el cluster completo**. Recompila únicamente el contenedor del bot para preservar la conexión de WhatsApp activa:
> ```bash
> docker compose -f docker-compose-prod.yml up -d --build whatsapp_bot_sismo
> ```

### Paso 3: Emparejar WhatsApp
1. Accede a tu panel de FZap Manager (`http://TU_IP:8081`).
2. Selecciona la instancia del bot (`FZAP_INSTANCE`).
3. Escanea el código QR de emparejamiento desde la aplicación móvil de WhatsApp (Dispositivos vinculados -> Vincular un dispositivo).

---

## Comandos del Bot (Uso en WhatsApp)

El bot procesa dinámicamente los siguientes mensajes enviados por los usuarios:

*   **Menú de Bienvenida y Ayuda**: Al enviar `hola`, `ayuda`, `help`, `/ayuda`, `/help` o `#`, se muestra el instructivo del bot.
*   **Búsqueda de Personas**: Envía el nombre completo de la persona o su Cédula de Identidad (mínimo 4 números) para buscar registros confirmados en hospitales y refugios de forma unificada en ambas redes de búsqueda (Localizados VE + SOS Venezuela).
*   **Búsqueda de Refugios**: Envía la palabra `refugio` sola para ver todos los albergues, o incluye la ciudad (ej: `refugio en Caracas` o `refugio La Guaira`) para ver los específicos de una localidad.
*   **Centros de Donación/Acopio**: Envía la palabra `acopio` o `donar` seguida de la ciudad (ej: `acopio en Valencia`) para ubicar centros de recolección de víveres.
*   **Números de Emergencia**: Envía la palabra `emergencia` o `telefono` para obtener el directorio telefónico de ayuda.

analysis.summary: A lean, stateless WhatsApp chatbot built with Node.js + Fzap API that aggregates person-finder, shelter, donation-center, and emergency data from multiple Venezuelan earthquake relief APIs (localizadosvenezuela.com, sosvenezuela2026.com, acopiove.org, responsegrid.app). The bot is apparently live (homepage: wa.me/584225252437). Code quality is high for a 2-day-old hackathon project: proper security hardening, per-JID task queue for race condition prevention, minor-data protection, timing-safe auth, and a well-structured Docker setup. Key weaknesses: zero tests, no CI, default credentials baked into compose files, and one suspicious hardcoded test UUID in the ResponseGrid API call.
analysis.project_type: WhatsApp chatbot / humanitarian API aggregator

database: PostgreSQL 15 (used only by Fzap API, not the bot itself)

architecture.pattern: Stateless microservice / API aggregator bot
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No local database. The bot itself is entirely stateless — it only uses an in-memory TTL Map for response caching. PostgreSQL is used exclusively by the Fzap WhatsApp bridge container.
architecture.api_design: Receives inbound messages via POST /webhook/messages from Fzap API (webhook pattern); calls 4 external REST APIs (localizadosvenezuela.com, sosvenezuela2026.com, acopiove.org, responsegrid.app) using axios with Promise.allSettled for fault tolerance.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: src/webhook/auth.js (auth middleware), src/webhook/parser.js (payload normalization), src/webhook/handler.js (dispatcher), src/sismoRouter.js (business logic), src/services/fzap.js (send), src/services/fzapProvision.js (startup provisioning), src/utils/cache.js, src/utils/taskQueue.js.
architecture.modularity: Good for a small project. sismoRouter.js is 530 LOC and handles all intents in a single file — could be split per domain but is still readable.
notable_patterns: Per-JID sequential task queue to prevent race conditions on burst messages
Promise.allSettled dual-API fan-out with client-side deduplication and merge by normalized name
TTL in-memory Map cache to reduce upstream API pressure
Idempotent startup provisioning (check before create/register webhook)
Minor-data protection: location and notes hidden for persons under 18
Timing-safe (crypto.timingSafeEqual) webhook secret comparison
patch.js: runtime binary patch on Fzap API to handle @lid JID resolution

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ split into webhook/, services/, utils/ with clear single-responsibility files. Root holds Dockerfile, two compose files, and .env.example.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive names throughout (routeMessage, parseMessage, enqueue, provisionFzap). Spanish comments match the project's Spanish-language domain.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Well-documented README with command table, env var table, and deployment steps. JSDoc on key functions. Inline comments explain non-obvious decisions (e.g., @lid JID fallback, taskQueue GC).
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is small (~1100 LOC) but consistently well-organized and documented, with a clear rationale for each module. The one gripe is that sismoRouter.js lumps all 5 intents into one 530-line file.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: FZAP_WEBHOOK_SECRET is correctly loaded from env in code, but docker-compose-prod.yml hardcodes it as 'webhook_secret_secure_sismo' and Postgres password is hardcoded as 'fzap123' in both compose files — operators must override these manually.
production_readiness.security_notes: Positives: Helmet headers, 120 req/min rate limiter, timing-safe HMAC comparison, health endpoint locked to localhost, non-root Docker user, JSON body limit 5 MB, minor-data location masking. Negatives: default credentials in compose files; fzap.js logs err.response?.status on send failure (low risk); the auth.js IP subnet fallback is acknowledged as insecure but present.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Stateless bot scales horizontally, but the in-memory cache and taskQueue are per-process — multiple replicas would lose cache coherence and each would maintain separate JID queues. Acceptable for single-instance deployment; would need Redis for multi-replica.
production_readiness.reasoning: Good security hygiene for a hackathon — more hardening than most submissions. Docker setup is solid. The hardcoded compose secrets and zero test coverage are the main gaps preventing a 4.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, functioning bot with genuine integration logic, privacy features, and an active deployment. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 8 commits, 1 contributor, project is 2 days old. Code quality exceeds typical hackathon submissions and the bot appears live, but the complete absence of tests and CI, combined with the brand-new status, caps the maturity score.

viability.reasoning: The bot is apparently deployed and running (real WhatsApp number in homepage). It solves a concrete, immediate need (family reunification + shelter/aid logistics via WhatsApp — the most accessible channel in Venezuela). Stateless design keeps operational complexity low. The main viability risk is full dependency on 4 external APIs that are not under the team's control, and one ResponseGrid call uses a UUID that appears to be a test/placeholder ID (11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111), which may return no real data.

key_features: Dual-source person search: queries localizadosvenezuela.com and sosvenezuela2026.com in parallel, deduplicates and merges results by normalized name
Shelter finder: queries acopiove.org by city, showing occupancy status and immediate needs
Donation center finder: same AcopioVE endpoint filtered by tipo=acopio
Urgent needs/supplies: queries responsegrid.app public emergencies endpoint with local city filtering
Emergency phone directory: fetches from acopiove.org/v1/telefonos with 5-minute cache
Minor data protection: location and notes suppressed for persons under 18
Cédula masking: only shows first 2 and last 3 digits
Per-JID sequential task queue preventing race conditions on burst messages
Idempotent Fzap provisioning on startup
In-memory TTL cache (1–5 min) to reduce upstream API load
analysis.merge_potential: Medium-high. The bot is a thin aggregator over external APIs with no local data store, so merging its person-finder or shelter-search logic into another platform would require exposing those same external API calls through a different interface. The clean service/router separation makes extraction feasible.
red_flags: ResponseGrid API call uses a hardcoded placeholder UUID (11111111-1111-4111-8111-111111111111) — this endpoint almost certainly returns no real emergency data in production
FZAP_WEBHOOK_SECRET is hardcoded as 'webhook_secret_secure_sismo' in docker-compose-prod.yml — operators may not realize they must change it
PostgreSQL password hardcoded as 'fzap123' in both compose files
In-memory cache and task queue are per-process — multiple instances would be incoherent
Full runtime dependency on 4 external APIs not operated by this team (localizadosvenezuela.com, sosvenezuela2026.com, acopiove.org, responsegrid.app)
Zero automated tests
No CI/CD pipeline

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The bot directly addresses the three most urgent needs after an earthquake: finding missing persons, locating active shelters, and finding donation/aid centers. It does this over WhatsApp — the single most accessible communication channel in Venezuela, requiring no app download, no account creation, and no data literacy beyond sending a text message. It aggregates four real relief APIs (localizadosvenezuela.com, sosvenezuela2026.com, acopiove.org, responsegrid.app) with fault-tolerant parallel queries, smart deduplication, and minor-data privacy protections. This is not a concept — there is a live WhatsApp number (+584225252437) with a profile named "Terremoto Venezuela" accepting messages right now.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: wa.me/584225252437 resolves to a live WhatsApp contact page with a "Terremoto Venezuela" profile and an active phone number. Any user can message it now. A YouTube Shorts demo video is also present (https://youtube.com/shorts/hB-zcKp5Cak). The only caveat: one of the five data sources (ResponseGrid urgent-needs) uses a hardcoded placeholder UUID in the API call, so that feature may silently return empty data in production. The remaining four features (person finder, shelter finder, donation centers, emergency directory) appear fully wired to real APIs.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the WhatsApp phone number as a call to action. Emphasize zero friction — no app, no account, just send a message with a name or cédula. Pair with the YouTube Shorts demo showing a real search. Target diaspora networks on Twitter/X, Instagram, and Telegram who are desperately trying to locate family members inside Venezuela.
gaps: No screenshots available for static social posts
No branding assets (logo, colors)
No confirmed real users or testimonials yet
ResponseGrid (urgent needs feature) may silently fail due to placeholder UUID

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Envía un nombre o cédula por WhatsApp y encuentra a tu familiar desaparecido — LocalizadosVzlaBot busca en tiempo real en todos los hospitales, refugios y redes comunitarias del terremoto de Venezuela 2026.

name: Bloom-Sismo
db_description: La idea consiste en crear una Progressive Web App (PWA) de una sola página HTML que pesa menos de 150 KB en total y que contiene un Filtro de Bloom de todas las personas reportadas como Localizadas o A salvo en las distintas plataformas unificadas.

analysis.summary: Bloom-Sismo is a single-file PWA (~90-150 KB) that embeds a pre-compiled Bloom Filter of earthquake victims reported as located/safe, enabling zero-latency offline lookup by Venezuelan ID number (cédula). The filter is built from CSV/JSON sources via a Node.js build pipeline and injected as base64 into index.html, so the entire app — search, offline report queue, and QR sharing — works without any network connection. A Supabase REST endpoint receives missing-person reports (with IndexedDB offline queue + Background Sync). The project has a working deployed demo, but currently contains only 20 demo records with no real NGO data connected.
analysis.project_type: Static PWA / JAMstack — single HTML file with embedded Bloom Filter, Supabase REST for report ingestion, and a Node.js build pipeline

architecture.pattern: Static PWA / JAMstack with build-time filter compilation
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase REST API calls via native fetch — no SDK, no ORM. IndexedDB accessed directly via idbOp helper.
architecture.api_design: No server API. Client POSTs missing-person reports to Supabase REST /rest/v1/reports with anon key. Filter data is embedded in HTML as base64 constants at build time.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean split between build-time (build-filter.js, fetch-ong-api.js compile data and inject into HTML) and runtime (index.html + sw.js). The intentional monolithic single-file output enables WhatsApp distribution.
architecture.modularity: Intentionally low modularity in the output artifact (single HTML file by design). Build pipeline is modular: separate scripts for fetching ONG data, compiling filter, and verifying.
notable_patterns: Bloom Filter with FNV-1a double-hash (k=10, m≈89KB, p=0.001)
Stale-While-Revalidate Service Worker strategy
Fire-and-forget Supabase POST with AbortController 8s timeout
IndexedDB offline queue drained via Background Sync API
Base64 filter injection via regex replacement into HTML marker comment
Lazy QR code script loading from CDN

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root with index.html, sw.js, build-filter.js, fetch-ong-api.js, verify.js, sample-data.json, vercel.json, datos/ folder, and .github/workflows/. Clean and logical given the project's single-file output goal.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming throughout (cedula, fuente, localizados, buscar, reportar). Constants and functions are descriptive. Build scripts use box-drawing comment headers for section navigation.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is comprehensive: explains the math, data format, ONG integration flow, Supabase SQL schema, privacy model, and deployment steps. Inline comments in all scripts are thorough. Marks TODO/PENDING sections clearly.
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for a deliberately minimal codebase. The single-file constraint is a design choice justified by the WhatsApp distribution requirement, not laziness. The build scripts are polished enough to be handed to a non-technical NGO volunteer.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Supabase anon key (sb_publishable_...) hardcoded in index.html and committed to Git. The key prefix indicates it is intentionally a public publishable key, and the Supabase RLS schema in the README restricts reads to authenticated users. However, it is still an exposed credential in version history.
production_readiness.security_notes: vercel.json sets strong security headers (X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-XSS-Protection, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, COOP, COEP). RLS on Supabase restricts report reads to authenticated users. The Bloom Filter is one-way — cédulas cannot be reconstructed from it. UUID v4 idempotency prevents duplicate reports.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: The core lookup is purely client-side with zero server load — scales to unlimited concurrent users. Supabase handles report ingestion horizontally with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. Service Worker ensures the CDN-cached HTML serves all traffic without origin hits.
production_readiness.reasoning: App is deployed and the technical architecture is sound for scale, but it has zero real data (20 demo records only), the ONG API integration is a placeholder, and the CI filter-rebuild step is commented out. It cannot fulfill its purpose until real NGO data is wired in.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, purpose-built project with genuine algorithmic logic (Bloom Filter, FNV-1a hash, IndexedDB offline queue, Background Sync). Not boilerplate. However, it contains only demo data and the critical NGO data pipeline is unconnected.
maturity.reasoning: Created June 27, 2026 — only 1-2 days old with 11 commits and 2 contributors. verify.js is a useful manual CLI tool but not an automated test suite. The CI deploy.yml exists but has the filter-compilation step commented out, meaning deployed HTML always reflects the last manually compiled filter.

viability.reasoning: The core algorithm and PWA architecture are technically correct and well-suited to the low-bandwidth, high-volume Venezuela earthquake use case. The blocker is entirely data: no real NGO partnership is formalized, the ONG_CONFIG.endpoint is a placeholder URL, and the datos/ folder contains only test CSV/JSON files. If a real data feed can be established with any of the named NGOs (Venezuela Te Busca, Cruz Roja Venezuela), the app could be operational within hours given the existing build pipeline.

key_features: Offline Bloom Filter lookup: zero-latency, zero-network cédula search embedded as base64 in HTML
Service Worker with Stale-While-Revalidate strategy — works fully in airplane mode
IndexedDB offline queue for missing-person report submission with Background Sync drain
Node.js build pipeline: reads CSV/JSON from datos/ → compiles Bloom Filter → injects into index.html
Supabase REST integration with 8s AbortController timeout and UUID v4 idempotency
QR code sharing (lazy CDN load) for distributing updated filter between phones without data
WhatsApp-distributable: entire app is a single HTML file under 150 KB
Security headers configured in vercel.json (X-Frame-Options, COOP, COEP, Permissions-Policy)
analysis.merge_potential: Medium — the Bloom Filter PWA shell is a genuinely novel offline lookup layer that could complement heavier platforms as a lightweight satellite app. The build pipeline is clean and extensible. However, the missing data integration (NGO API) is the entire unsolved problem, and the CI build step being commented out means any merge would require re-enabling automated filter updates.
red_flags: No real missing-persons data: filter contains only 20 demo records — zero operational value until NGO data is ingested
ONG API endpoint is a placeholder string ('https://API-DE-LA-ONG.org/v1/localizados') — integration not started
CI filter-compilation step is commented out in deploy.yml — deployed app will never auto-update the filter on push
Supabase anon key committed to Git history (sb_publishable_... — publishable by design, but still a credential in public repo)
datos/ folder is gitignored with only placeholder files — operational data workflow requires manual steps outside version control
No automated tests — verify.js is a manual CLI tool requiring human interpretation, not a test runner

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The concept is genuinely well-targeted: searching whether a family member has been reported safe after an earthquake, fully offline, distributable via WhatsApp as a single 150 KB HTML file. That is a real, urgent need in a low-connectivity disaster scenario. However, the app currently contains only 20 synthetic demo records and the NGO data integration endpoint is a literal placeholder string ('https://API-DE-LA-ONG.org/v1/localizados'). Until real data from Venezuela Te Busca, Cruz Roja Venezuela, or another credible source is wired in, the app cannot locate a single actual victim — it will return "not found" for everyone, which is worse than useless for a family member searching in panic.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The listed live_url points to the GitHub repository page, not a deployed web app. The README mentions Vercel and GitHub Pages as deployment targets and the vercel.json is configured, but no publicly reachable deployed URL is provided in the project metadata. Even if a Vercel deployment exists, it would contain only 20 demo records with no real data value. No demo video was submitted.

diffusion.recommended_angle: If the team can secure even a partial CSV feed from one NGO and deploy a live URL, this becomes a genuinely promotable story: 'the app that fits in a WhatsApp message and tells you if your family is safe.' Hold promotion until then — the technical innovation is real, the product is not yet operational.
gaps: no-live-demo
no-demo-video
no-screenshots
no-real-data-zero-operational-value
no-ngo-partnership-formalized
no-branding-assets

evaluation.one_line_pitch: A 150 KB offline PWA that lets anyone search if their loved one has been reported safe after an earthquake — shareable via WhatsApp with zero internet required.

name: Psico Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
# Red de Apoyo Psicológico Venezuela

Plataforma de apoyo psicológico para respuesta a emergencias en Venezuela. Conecta a personas que necesitan contención emocional con psicólogos verificados, y permite a los profesionales registrarse, subir sus credenciales y gestionar su disponibilidad.

**Producción:** [psicoayudaven.com](https://psicoayudaven.com)

## Características

- **Ruta de paciente** (`/ayuda`): acceso rápido a ayuda presencial o a distancia.
- **Directorio de profesionales** (`/ayuda/profesionales`): psicólogos verificados con estado en línea y contacto directo por WhatsApp. Los disponibles aparecen primero. Grilla de 2 columnas en escritorio.
- **Perfil profesional** (`/ayuda/profesionales/$id`): página pública por profesional con SEO (Open Graph, Twitter Cards, JSON-LD) y botón **Compartir** (Web Share API → portapapeles).
- **Registro de profesionales** (`/profesional/registro`): formulario en dos pasos. La credencial es un único número de colegiación agnóstico de país (+ colegio certificador opcional). Soporta psicólogos dentro y fuera de Venezuela.
- **Panel del profesional** (`/profesional/panel`): activar/desactivar disponibilidad ("En línea" / "No conectado").
- **Panel de administración** (`/admin`): revisar y verificar o rechazar registros. Acceso basado en base de datos (columna `user.role`).
- **Centro de cuenta** (`/cuenta`): hub según rol (login, panel, admin, cerrar sesión).
- **SEO** en todas las páginas públicas (títulos, descripciones, Open Graph, canonical vía `src/lib/seo.ts`).
- **Autenticación** con Better Auth (email + contraseña).
- UI _mobile-first_ con estética _liquid glass_ sobre la paleta Medicall. Navegación: barra inferior en móvil, barra superior en escritorio.

analysis.summary: psico-support (psicoayudaven.com) is a real, working psychological support directory platform built with TanStack Start (React SSR) deployed on Cloudflare Workers with D1 SQLite via Drizzle ORM. It provides a searchable, paginated directory of verified psychologists who can be contacted directly via WhatsApp, with a professional self-registration flow, admin approval workflow, and availability toggling. The codebase shows genuine, intentional design decisions documented throughout — this is not boilerplate. The domain psicoayudaven.com appears in the wrangler config, indicating real deployment intent. Created 2 days before the hackathon deadline with 24 commits and active schema iteration (7 migration files).
analysis.project_type: Full-stack SSR web app (TanStack Start) deployed to Cloudflare Workers edge runtime

architecture.pattern: SSR full-stack with co-located server functions (no separate API server)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM over Cloudflare D1 (SQLite). DB connection is lazily initialised from the Cloudflare env binding set per-request via setCloudflareEnv(); a module-level cached singleton prevents repeated initialisation. Local dev uses better-sqlite3.
architecture.api_design: TanStack Start createServerFn RPCs — typed, co-located with route modules, validated with Zod. No REST/GraphQL surface. All auth-gated operations re-verify the session on every call via better-auth + DB role lookup.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: src/server/ holds all server-side logic (professionals.ts, locations.ts); src/db/ holds schema + connection; src/routes/ holds page components and their loaders/beforeLoad guards; src/lib/ holds cross-cutting utilities (auth, notifications, seo, env).
architecture.modularity: Good — shared WHERE builder (buildProfessionalWhere), shared insert builder (buildProValues), and shared Zod schemas prevent drift between list, random-pick, and registration paths.
notable_patterns: Admin role stored as DB column (not env list), looked up per-request — role changes take effect without redeploy
Paginated professional listing with shared WHERE clause reused by 'contact random' feature to guarantee consistent filter semantics
WhatsApp is the sole contact mechanism — no in-app messaging, no email blast
SSR loader pre-fetches first page then suspense query with refetchInterval:20s keeps availability status live
Population demographics stored as JSON text array with LIKE filter — acceptable for small table, noted in comments
Seven migration files showing active schema iteration (collapsed Venezuelan credential fields from 4 columns to 1 country-agnostic field)

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear: src/server/ (business logic), src/db/ (ORM), src/routes/ (file-based routing with TanStack), src/lib/ (utilities), src/components/ (UI). Drizzle migrations in drizzle/. Public assets in public/.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — Spanish route paths (/ayuda, /profesional) match the target audience; English identifiers in code; Zod schemas named *Schema; server fns named after their action.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually high for a hackathon — extensive 'ponytail:' inline comments on every non-obvious design decision explaining the why, not just the what. AGENTS.md and docs/ui-style.md present.
code_organization.reasoning: Code quality significantly exceeds typical hackathon submissions. Intentional tradeoffs are explicitly documented (e.g., ORDER BY RANDOM() acceptable while <1k rows, LIKE filter can't use index). Consistent TypeScript throughout with Zod validation on all server function inputs.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: BETTER_AUTH_SECRET in .env.example (not hardcoded). D1 binding and database_id committed to wrangler.jsonc (D1 IDs are not secrets, but the database_name and ID are public). Sentry DSN is optional via VITE_SENTRY_DSN.
production_readiness.security_notes: Admin operations re-check DB role on every server fn call — no stale JWT privilege escalation. Public listing only returns verified professionals. WhatsApp numbers are publicly visible in the directory listing with no auth wall — any scraper can harvest them. No rate limiting on any endpoint. Email verification is not enforced before professional registration (account creation immediately creates a 'pending' row that admins must approve — this is intentional but means anyone can occupy the pending queue).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Cloudflare Workers + D1 is edge-distributed and auto-scales. D1 is eventually consistent on replicas but reads on Workers are handled by the primary unless using read replicas. LIKE '%...%' filters and ORDER BY RANDOM() are fine at hackathon scale (<1k rows) but would need indexing strategy if the directory grows to tens of thousands.
production_readiness.reasoning: Strong fundamentals — auth, role-based access, Sentry integration, Zod validation, friendly error messages that never leak raw SQL. Missing: no CI pipeline, no tests, no rate limiting, and sendDefaultPii:true in Sentry logs user IPs (potential privacy concern for vulnerable earthquake victims).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — seven DB migrations show active schema evolution over the project's two-day life (collapsed Venezuelan credential fields, added country/estado/ciudad, added role column). The wrangler.jsonc contains an actual D1 database_id and custom domain (psicoayudaven.com). Server functions have production-quality error handling and user-facing Spanish messages.
maturity.reasoning: Clearly a real working MVP, not scaffolding. However, 24 commits, 1 contributor, no tests, and no CI pipeline are significant gaps for a system handling vulnerable users' contact information. The vitest dependency is declared but no test files exist.

viability.reasoning: Strong viability: the core user flow (find a verified psychologist by location/modality, tap to open WhatsApp) is fully implemented and deployable to Cloudflare today. The admin moderation workflow is complete. The WhatsApp-as-contact-layer is pragmatic for Venezuela's connectivity constraints. The main gap is building the professional supply side — the platform is useless without a critical mass of registered, admin-approved psychologists, and there is no outreach or seeding mechanism in the codebase.

key_features: Searchable, paginated directory of verified psychologists filterable by modality (in-person/remote), Venezuelan state, city, and population demographic
Professional self-registration with two-step form (account creation + credential details)
Admin approval panel: review pending professionals, approve/reject, promote users to admin role
'Contact random professional' button — picks from the same filtered pool as the current list view and opens WhatsApp
Professional availability toggle (self-reported 'En línea' status)
Per-professional public profile pages with SSR SEO head tags
PWA support (service worker, manifest, maskable icons)
Sentry error monitoring (optional, via VITE_SENTRY_DSN)
Role-based access: admin role stored in DB, checked per-request without redeploy
analysis.merge_potential: Low — this is a standalone, domain-specific mental health professional directory. It does not expose data APIs or webhooks that other projects could consume. Could theoretically link into a broader aid portal but would require significant integration work.
red_flags: No tests despite vitest being configured — zero confidence in regression safety
No CI/CD pipeline — deployments are manual `npm run build && wrangler deploy`
WhatsApp numbers are publicly accessible in the directory with no authentication wall — creates a scraping/spam vector targeting professionals
No rate limiting on any server function — professional registration, random-pick, and listing are all unthrottled
Sentry configured with sendDefaultPii:true — logs IP addresses of users who may be earthquake victims seeking mental health help (privacy risk)
Email verification is not enforced — professionals can register and occupy the admin queue with unverified emails
Single contributor with 24 commits over 2 days — bus factor of 1
i18n messages exist only in de.json and en.json, but the app UI is entirely in Spanish — the i18n infrastructure appears unused

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Mental health crises are a documented, underserved secondary disaster after earthquakes. Venezuela's existing mental health infrastructure is already strained before any seismic event. This platform directly addresses the gap by connecting affected people to verified psychologists via WhatsApp — the dominant communication channel in Venezuela even on limited data plans. The need is real, immediate, and ongoing after any earthquake event. The WhatsApp-first contact model is a deliberate, pragmatic choice for Venezuela's connectivity reality rather than a shortcut.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: psicoayudaven.com loads cleanly with a professional landing page offering two clear CTAs (seek help / register as psychologist). The /ayuda/profesionales directory is live and populated with 4 real psychologist profiles including actual Venezuelan names, locations (Maracay, Los Teques, Caracas), specializations by patient demographic, availability status, and functional WhatsApp contact links with pre-filled messages. End-to-end user flow works in production today.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Free, verified psychologist network connecting earthquake-affected Venezuelans directly via WhatsApp — no app download, no cost, no barriers. Built in 48 hours by a Venezuelan developer.
gaps: No demo video showing the user flow
Only 4 professionals in the directory — hard to claim 'network' scale in promotional copy
No screenshots prepared for social media
No testimonials or real-user social proof
Professional supply-side growth mechanism is entirely manual (outreach not automated)

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Psico Ayuda Venezuela conecta a víctimas del terremoto con psicólogos venezolanos verificados — gratis, confidencial y accesible por WhatsApp en segundos.

name: Rastro
db_description:
# Rastro - Plataforma de Respuesta a Emergencias

Rastro es una plataforma de respuesta a emergencias post-terremoto, diseñada para facilitar la comunicación y logística en situaciones críticas. Está optimizada para ofrecer fricción mínima (no requiere registro para reportar) y garantizar un flujo de información verificada.

## 🎯 ¿Para qué sirve?

La aplicación está dividida en tres módulos principales:

1. **Mapa Colaborativo (`/mapa`):** Muestra reportes geolocalizados en tiempo real sobre necesidades de rescate, suministros y estado de vías.
2. **Buscador de Personas (`/personas`):** Permite registrar y buscar personas en refugios (por nombre o cédula de identidad), facilitando la reunificación familiar.
3. **Panel de Moderación (`/moderacion`):** Un área restringida donde los moderadores revisan, aprueban o rechazan los reportes ciudadanos antes de publicarlos en el mapa, evitando desinformación.

## 🛠️ Stack Tecnológico

- **Framework:** [Next.js 14](https://nextjs.org/) (App Router)
- **Lenguaje:** [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- **Estilos:** [Tailwind CSS](https://tailwindcss.com/)
- **Base de Datos, Auth, Storage y Realtime:** [Supabase](https://supabase.com/)
- **Mapas:** [Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) + `react-leaflet` + OpenStreetMap
- **Formularios y Validación:** `react-hook-form` + `zod`
- **Búsqueda:** `fuse.js` (Búsqueda fuzzy)
- **Íconos:** `lucide-react`
- **Hosting y Cron Jobs:** [Vercel](https://vercel.com/)

analysis.summary: Rastro is a post-earthquake emergency response platform built with Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript, Supabase, and Leaflet. It covers three core workflows: a public collaborative map showing geo-tagged rescue/supply/road reports, a shelter-based people registry with fuzzy search for family reunification, and a cookie-authenticated moderator panel for approving or rejecting citizen reports before they go live. The app is deployed and accessible at rastro-flax.vercel.app. Code is real, purposeful, and well-structured for a 5-commit hackathon project.
analysis.project_type: Web application — emergency response platform (Next.js full-stack)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router full-stack — server Route Handlers as API layer, client components for interactivity
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js v2) — two clients: anon key for public reads, service-role key for writes/admin ops; no ORM
architecture.api_design: REST-style Next.js Route Handlers under /app/api/* — separate endpoints for reportes, personas, refugios, moderacion, and a cron endpoint; Zod validation on every POST/PATCH before DB write
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: src/lib (supabase clients, validations, utils), src/types (domain types), src/hooks (data fetching hooks), src/app/api (server), src/components (UI)
architecture.modularity: Good — each page has a server wrapper and a client component; shared Zod schemas used in both API and forms via zodResolver
notable_patterns: Two Supabase clients: anon for public reads (enforces RLS), service-role admin for server-side writes (bypasses RLS intentionally)
Zod schemas shared between server validation and react-hook-form client validation
RLS policies enforce estado=pendiente on public inserts and restrict reads to published/sin_verificar states
PLpgSQL trigger for updated_at, atomic upvote function via SECURITY DEFINER
Vercel Cron auto-archives reports older than 7 days via Bearer token check
Moderation auth via shared-secret cookie (httpOnly, sameSite=lax, 8h TTL)

code_organization.directory_structure: Logical and clean: src/app (pages + API routes), src/components (ui + mapa), src/hooks, src/lib, src/types, supabase/schema.sql at root. Mirrors Next.js App Router conventions well.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — Spanish domain names (reportar, personas, moderacion, refugios) are consistent throughout types, DB schema, API paths, and UI labels; no naming drift
code_organization.documentation_quality: Good — README explains all three modules, setup steps, and tech stack; AGENTS.md and MANUAL.md present; inline comments on every route handler explaining intent and security constraints
code_organization.reasoning: For a 5-commit hackathon project the code is unusually clean and well-organized. Types, validation schemas, and DB schema are all in sync. The one gap is two RPC functions (buscar_personas, incrementar_personas_count) referenced in code but absent from schema.sql.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service role key kept server-side only (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, never prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_). MODERACION_SECRET and CRON_SECRET also server-only. .env.example committed with blank values and comments warning against client exposure.
production_readiness.security_notes: Moderation auth is a shared-secret cookie, not proper user auth — anyone who learns the secret can moderate. The moderado_por field is hardcoded as 'moderador' so there is no identity tracking per moderator. No rate limiting on public POST /api/reportes or /api/personas — spam/flood risk. Cron route has a bug: the Bearer token check is only applied in production (NODE_ENV === 'production'), allowing unauthenticated local triggering.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase handles DB scaling. No pagination on /api/personas (hard limit of 100 rows). Realtime map updates depend on Supabase Realtime subscriptions. Vercel serverless functions scale automatically. No CDN/cache layer for read-heavy map endpoint.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is live and structurally sound: RLS, env separation, error handling, and cron archiving are all real. Gaps that matter at scale are: missing rate limiting, single shared-secret moderation auth, two DB functions referenced but not in schema.sql (buscar_personas, incrementar_personas_count), and no error monitoring (Sentry/etc).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — contains domain-specific logic, a real DB schema with seed data, working API routes with validation, and a live Vercel deployment. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: Created June 28, 2026 with only 5 commits and 1 contributor — clearly hackathon-fresh. Zero tests and no CI pipeline. However, the functionality that exists is real and works: the deployed URL is active, the schema is complete, and the three modules are all implemented end-to-end.

viability.reasoning: The three modules (geomap, people finder, moderation) directly address the most acute post-earthquake needs: locating victims, tracking people in shelters, and preventing misinformation. The no-registration-required report submission minimizes friction in crisis. Main risks to production viability are: spam/abuse with no rate limiting, two missing DB functions that will cause runtime errors (buscar_personas fallback, incrementar_personas_count), and a single-moderator shared-secret auth model that doesn't scale to a real moderation team.

key_features: Public geo-tagged report submission (rescue, supply, road) without account registration
Moderated content pipeline: reports go pendiente → publicado/sin_verificar/rechazado before appearing on public map
Interactive Leaflet map with clickable report markers and upvote system
Shelter-based people registry with fuzzy name search (fuse.js) and cedula-based lookup
Family contact information stored per person in shelter for reunification
Cookie-authenticated moderator panel with approve/reject/unverified actions and optional notes
Vercel Cron job auto-archives reports older than 7 days at 3am UTC daily
Row Level Security on all tables: public inserts locked to pendiente state, reads filtered to published states only
Dual Supabase client pattern: anon key for public reads, service-role for all writes
analysis.merge_potential: Medium-high. The three modules are cleanly separated and could be integrated into a larger platform as independent features. The Supabase schema, Zod types, and API contracts are well-defined. Would need rate limiting and proper multi-user auth before merging into a shared moderation workflow.
red_flags: Two PostgreSQL RPC functions referenced in code (buscar_personas, incrementar_personas_count) are absent from supabase/schema.sql — will produce runtime errors on the fallback search path and after every person registration
No rate limiting on any public POST endpoint — /api/reportes and /api/personas are open to flood attacks
Moderation auth is a single shared secret stored in a cookie — no per-moderator identity, no audit trail of who approved what, secret rotation requires redeploying all moderator sessions
Cron route authorization check is guarded by NODE_ENV === 'production' check — in development the route is completely open to unauthenticated calls
No test coverage at all (unit, integration, or e2e)
No CI pipeline — no automated build verification on push
Photo attachment is a free-text URL field with no upload/storage integration — users must host images externally, which is unreliable in a disaster scenario
personas SELECT RLS policy is USING (TRUE) — all person records including health status and family phone numbers are publicly readable by anyone with the anon key

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Rastro targets the three most acute post-earthquake coordination failures: (1) locating victims and reporting rescue needs in real time via a moderated geo-map, (2) reunifying families separated across shelters via a cedula/name search registry, and (3) fighting misinformation by requiring moderator approval before reports go public. No account registration is needed to submit a report, which is exactly the right design for a crisis where people have lost their phones or IDs. These are not hypothetical features — all three modules are implemented end-to-end with a real Supabase schema and live deployment.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The app loads at https://rastro-six.vercel.app/mapa and https://rastro-six.vercel.app/personas. Navigation, layout, and UI elements are fully rendered. However the map shows "Cargando reportes… / Cargando mapa…" with zero report counts — the database appears empty (no seed data), so a first-time visitor sees a blank map. The personas search page is visually complete and ready to accept queries, but returns no results with no existing entries. The product is structurally working but not demonstrable without pre-loaded data. Two missing PostgreSQL RPC functions (buscar_personas, incrementar_personas_count) will cause runtime errors on the people-search fallback path and after person registration.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the family reunification angle — 'Find your loved ones in shelters by name or cedula, no app download required.' Show all three tabs in a short screen recording with a few seeded demo entries so the map and people search look alive. Emphasize the moderation pipeline as the trust differentiator vs. unverified social media.
gaps: No demo video — the empty-database live demo does not convey the product's power
No seed/demo data in the live deployment — map shows zero reports, people search returns nothing
No screenshots in the repo or README showing a working state
No branding beyond the name Rastro
Privacy red flag (all person records publicly readable via anon key) must be fixed before any public promotion to avoid exposing victim data
No real users or testimonials yet

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Rastro lets earthquake survivors report rescue needs on a live map and helps families find loved ones in shelters by name or ID — no account required, every report verified before it goes public.

name: Reencuentros Terremotos Venezuela
db_description:
Nuestra propuesta de valor se basa en una plataforma que centraliza reportes visuales (fotos y videos) recientes y georreferenciados de quienes necesitan ser encontrados —personas y animales—. Esta información es capturada en el terreno por medios de comunicación acreditados e instituciones de ayuda autorizadas.
