Ponemos la inteligencia artificial al servicio de esta causa para procesar grandes volúmenes de datos e identificar coincidencias que faciliten reencuentros seguros y oportunos, asegurando que sean las propias instituciones y medios autorizados quienes validen cada hallazgo dentro del sistema para una gestión segura y privada de la información.

Importante: nuestro norte es tener completamente ajustados los protocolos de ciberseguridad, protección de datos sensibles, bienestar animal y no revictimización. Para nosotros, la seguridad de las personas y los animales siempre estará por encima de la velocidad de lanzamiento.

Reporte cierre de hackathon Build 4 Venezuela | 28-junio-2026
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ROkUtXL9PnpGrWa12x4zw6e7_sWmyfjEA_1AJ4x44QE/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.3o36mzss0moq

analysis.summary: ReencuentroTerremotoVenezuela is a full-stack missing-persons aggregation platform built for the Venezuela earthquake response. The backend is a TypeScript/Express API with MongoDB, Redis-backed BullMQ queues, MinIO media storage, and a multi-provider AI pipeline (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini). The frontend is a React 19 + Vite SPA with Leaflet maps and PWA support. The system scrapes and reconciles data from multiple Venezuelan humanitarian sources (FUNVISIS, USGS, VenezuelaReporta, DTV, Cruz Roja) into a unified person record, applies fuzzy-match deduplication and AI-assisted record enrichment, and exposes a searchable feed + geo map to end-users. There is a live Vercel deployment and 135 commits from one active contributor over ~48 hours of hackathon work.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web application — REST API backend + React SPA frontend

architecture.pattern: Layered monolith (routes → services → models) with async worker separation
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Mongoose v9 with typed schemas, geo indexes (2dsphere), compound indexes, and upsert-heavy write patterns. Raw bulkWrite used in sync jobs for performance.
architecture.api_design: REST API under /api/* prefix. Routes are modular (person, auth, admin, disasters, localizados, search-request, webhooks, partners, media, cne). Admin routes protected by middleware. JSON payloads validated with Zod. Pagination via limit/offset. Redis caching on hot endpoints (30–300s TTL). 202 Accepted pattern used for async processing.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear layering: routes handle HTTP, services contain business logic, models own schema/DB access, jobs handle external sync, workers handle async AI processing, queues manage BullMQ job dispatch. Adapters normalize external source data.
architecture.modularity: Backend: src/routes/, src/services/, src/models/, src/workers/, src/queues/, src/jobs/, src/adapters/, src/validators/ — each concern in its own directory. Frontend: pages/, components/, services/, store/ with co-located CSS.
notable_patterns: Multi-provider AI factory pattern (AnthropicProvider/OpenAIProvider/GeminiProvider behind IAIProvider interface)
Idempotent upsert with SHA-256 idHash keyed on (normalizedName, state, age) for deduplication
Fuzzy-match reconciliation with 85%/95% thresholds routing to manual audit queue vs auto-merge
BullMQ worker separation: IA-processor worker runs independently from API container so AI load doesn't block HTTP
Vector embedding pipeline with Pinecone / Atlas Vector Search / local cosine similarity fallback
Audit log model for legal traceability of case closures
Safe projection on public person GET to exclude PII (contactPerson, externalIds, phone)

code_organization.directory_structure: Monorepo with back/ and front/ cleanly separated. Backend organized into 8+ subdirectories by concern. Frontend follows pages/components/services/store conventions. Config, validators, utils each in own folder.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent: service files are verb-noun (person.service.ts, reconciliation.service.ts), models are noun.model.ts, routes are noun.route.ts. Spanish naming is consistent with Venezuelan context where appropriate.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average: DEVELOPERS.md, TDD.md, Integraciones.md, plus /Doc folder with PDF, DOCX specs, data model diagrams, and UX wireframes. Inline code comments are thorough and in Spanish.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is well above hackathon average in organization quality. The only deduction is the absence of CI configuration and some copy-paste patterns in the job files.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Partially safe: .env.example exists and secrets are read from environment. CRITICAL: JWT_SECRET has a hardcoded fallback 'super-secret-key-reencuentro-2024' in auth.middleware.ts, and GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID defaults to 'dummy-client-id' with a code path that bypasses Google token verification in auth.route.ts. ADMIN_API_KEY in .env.example is 'super-secret-admin-key-2026'. These must be fixed before production.
production_readiness.security_notes: Positives: Helmet, HPP, express-rate-limit (100 req/15min global), JWT RBAC, non-root Docker user, safe projection on GET /persons to hide PII. Negatives: JWT_SECRET hardcoded fallback allows token forgery if env var is omitted; Google auth bypass path in dev mode could leak to prod; CORS is wide-open (app.use(cors()) with no origin restriction).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Worker/API separation via BullMQ is solid. Redis caching reduces DB pressure on hot endpoints. However, the reconciliation service does a full table scan per Venezuelan state for fuzzy matching (findSimilarPersons loads all persons by state into memory), which will degrade under load. The local cosine similarity fallback in matcher.service.ts loads the entire persons collection into Node.js memory — not viable at scale.
production_readiness.reasoning: The infrastructure setup (Docker Compose, multi-stage Dockerfile, healthchecks, BullMQ workers) is production-grade for a VPS deployment. The critical flaw is the JWT_SECRET fallback and open CORS that must be fixed. The mock data fallback in reencuentro.job.ts (generating 250 fake persons when the real API is down) could pollute a production database if the external API is temporarily unreachable.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, purpose-built application with genuine domain logic. The reconciliation pipeline, AI provider factory, multi-source scraping jobs, audit trail, and matching service are all original implementations specific to this use case. Not AI-generated boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 135 commits in ~48 hours with 1 contributor. Unit tests exist in back/test/unit/ (8+ test files covering services, routes, middleware) using Jest with mongodb-memory-server and ioredis-mock. Frontend has vitest setup. However, there is no CI pipeline and test coverage is not comprehensive — some files have no tests. The live Vercel deployment and real data from venezuelareporta (personas-localizadas-venezuelareporta.csv in /Doc) confirm real operational use.

viability.reasoning: This project directly addresses the core family-reunification need for earthquake victims in Venezuela. It has a live deployment, integrates with real Venezuelan data sources (FUNVISIS, Venezuela Reporta, DTV), implements AI-assisted deduplication to prevent fragmented records, and exposes a mobile-friendly map + feed interface. The main risks before real deployment are the JWT secret fallback and the open CORS. The architecture can realistically be hardened in hours, not days.

key_features: Multi-source person aggregation from FUNVISIS, USGS, VenezuelaReporta, DTV, Cruz Roja, Protección Civil via background sync jobs
AI-assisted record enrichment (urgency scoring, name normalization, safe description) via pluggable Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini provider
Fuzzy-match deduplication pipeline with auto-merge at 95% similarity and manual audit queue at 85–94%
Vector embedding matching (Pinecone, Atlas Vector Search, or local cosine similarity) to cross-reference missing-person reports with search requests
Interactive Leaflet map with person pins and disaster event overlays
Google OAuth authentication with role-based access control (user, verifier, admin)
Admin dashboard for manual audit review and case management
Audit log trail for legal traceability of case closures
MinIO media storage for photo/video uploads
Infinite-scroll feed with debounced search and Redis-cached responses
Docker Compose full-stack orchestration with health checks and non-root containers
PWA support for offline-capable mobile use
analysis.merge_potential: High. The platform's open partner API (/api/partners, webhooks route), adapter pattern for external sources, and documented integration guide make it a natural hub for aggregating data from other hackathon projects. The UnifiedPerson schema is flexible enough to absorb records from shelter-mapping or medical-health platforms via the webhook or partner endpoints.
red_flags: JWT_SECRET has hardcoded fallback 'super-secret-key-reencuentro-2024' in auth.middleware.ts — if the env var is not set in production, any attacker can forge admin tokens
Google auth route has a code path that skips token verification when GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID is 'dummy-client-id', which could reach production if env var is omitted
CORS is fully open (app.use(cors()) with no origin whitelist) — any site can make credentialed requests
reencuentro.job.ts generates 250 fake persons as a fallback when external API is unreachable, which would pollute the production database with synthetic data
reconciliation.service.ts findSimilarPersons loads all persons by state into memory for fuzzy scan — will fail under production data volume
mock MinIO credentials (minioadmin/minioadmin) shipped in .env.example and docker-compose defaults — likely to remain in production deployments
No CI/CD pipeline — no automated test gate before deployments

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family reunification after a major earthquake is a life-or-death need. Venezuela has fragmented humanitarian data spread across FUNVISIS, Cruz Roja, DTV, and local NGOs, with no unified missing-persons index. This platform directly addresses that fragmentation by aggregating multi-source reports, deduplicating via AI fuzzy-matching, and exposing a geo-map feed. The domain is real, the data sources wired in are real (venezuelareporta CSV with actual localized persons in /Doc), and the use case — a family member searching for a relative — is exactly the critical gap after a seismic event in Venezuela.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The registered live_url points to a Google Docs TDD specification document ("TDD_Reencuentro VE"), not a working product. The technical analysis confirms a live Vercel frontend deployment exists, but its URL is not surfaced in the project metadata. There is no demo video. The product is architecturally real (135 commits, 9,362 LOC, Docker Compose, BullMQ workers, Pinecone, MinIO all wired), but a judge or donor clicking the listed URL gets a Google Doc browser-compatibility error, not the app.

diffusion.recommended_angle: The first AI-powered missing-persons reunification hub for Venezuela earthquake survivors — aggregates data from FUNVISIS, Cruz Roja, and local media in real time, deduplicates fragmented reports with multi-model AI, and puts every case on an interactive map so families can search from anywhere in the world.
gaps: Public live URL to the actual app (Vercel deployment exists but is not linked)
Demo video showing the search flow and map in action
Screenshots of the feed, map, and admin audit dashboard
Branding assets (logo, social card)
No second contributor or institutional partner visible yet — single-contributor signal limits credibility for donors

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Reencuentros Venezuela: an AI-powered platform that unifies missing-persons reports from across Venezuela's fragmented humanitarian network so earthquake survivors and their families can find each other — fast.

name: Reestructuramos - Inspeccion Estructural Post Sismo
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**Reestructuramos** es una PWA **offline-first** para la **evaluación rápida de seguridad estructural de edificios después de un terremoto**, construida para los sismos **M7.2 + M7.5 del 24 de junio de 2026** en Venezuela.

Cada edificio se clasifica con el sistema universal de tres carteles — **VERDE** (habitable), **AMARILLO** (uso restringido) y **ROJO** (inseguro) — siguiendo la metodología **ATC-20** adaptada a la norma venezolana **COVENIN 1756**.

Lo que la hace distinta:

- **100% sin conexión.** Tras un sismo la red celular colapsa: ese es el caso de uso central, no el borde. Todo se guarda primero en el teléfono (IndexedDB) y se sincroniza solo cuando vuelve la señal. Incluye caché de mapas offline.
- **Evidencia ligada al hallazgo.** Cada foto se ata al daño concreto (esta columna, esta grieta) con GPS + fecha/hora embebidos. Nada de "álbum suelto".
- **Nunca sobrescribe el historial.** Una re-inspección tras una réplica es un registro NUEVO; el QR del cartel muestra todo el historial del edificio en el tiempo.
- **Sugiere, no decide.** La app propone el cartel según los daños observados, pero el inspector confirma o anula con su criterio (ambos quedan registrados).
- **Mapa de daños en vivo** de la ciudad, con pines a color por cartel.
- **PDF de inspección** + **cartel imprimible con QR**, generados en el dispositivo (funcionan offline).
- **Guía de campo offline** (patrones de daño, árbol de decisión, glosario) + mini-inducción con quiz para habilitar inspectores.

## ¿Para quién es?

Inspectores en campo: **voluntarios, ingenieros y arquitectos**. Acceso público y anónimo, sin barreras — cualquiera entra desde su teléfono y empieza de inmediato. También sirve como herramienta de formación.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Le da a Protección Civil, FUNVISIS, el colegio de ingenieros y a los voluntarios un **instrumento común** para triar miles de edificios rápido, con datos confiables (GPS + timestamp, trazabilidad antifraude) y un **mapa de daños que coordina la respuesta** — incluso con la red caída. Diseñado "listo para aval", alineado a COVENIN 1756.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Abre **https://app.reestructuramos.com** en el teléfono (instálala: "Agregar a pantalla de inicio").
2. Haz la **inducción de 2 minutos** + el mini-quiz.
3. Toca **"Nueva inspección"**: fija la ubicación (GPS o toca el mapa), marca los peligros con el selector de severidad, mira el **cartel sugerido**, confírmalo y **finaliza**.
4. Genera el **PDF** y el **cartel imprimible con QR**.
5. **Pon el teléfono en modo avión y recarga la app** — sigue funcionando. Ese es el corazón del proyecto.

## Tecnología

Next.js 16 + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind v4 · IndexedDB (Dexie) con cola de sincronización propia y reintentos · Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Storage + Auth anónima + RLS) · Leaflet + OpenStreetMap con caché offline · service worker propio · PDF y QR en el cliente. Diseño "instrumento de ingeniería": tipografía IBM Plex, alto contraste para uso al sol.

analysis.summary: Reestructuramos is a fully-realized offline-first PWA for post-earthquake structural building safety inspection (ATC-20 / COVENIN 1756 — green/yellow/red placard triage). Built explicitly for the M7.2+M7.5 Venezuela earthquakes of June 24, 2026, it stores all data locally in IndexedDB (Dexie) first, then synchronizes to Supabase via a hand-written queue with exponential backoff when connectivity returns. Features include a multi-step inspection wizard, a pure algorithmic placard suggestion engine, AI-assisted crack classification via Gemini Vision (server-side), annotatable photo capture, GPS tagging, offline map tiles cached in IndexedDB via Leaflet, PDF/QR report export, a collaborative damage map, inspector onboarding with quiz, and a coordinator role with elevated permissions. There is a live deployment at app.reestructuramos.com. Code quality is high with thorough RLS policies and security headers, but there are zero automated tests and no CI pipeline.
analysis.project_type: PWA (Progressive Web App) — offline-first mobile-web inspection tool

ai_ml: Gemini 2.5 Flash Vision API (crack/damage classification, server-side)

architecture.pattern: Local-first / offline-first with BaaS sync
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (direct table/storage calls) for remote; Dexie (IndexedDB) as the authoritative local store with its own query API
architecture.api_design: Minimal server surface: one Next.js API route (/api/analizar-grieta) protects the Gemini API key. All other CRUD goes directly from browser to Supabase via RLS-protected client. No traditional REST or GraphQL layer.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation: lib/db.ts owns local persistence, lib/sync.ts owns the queue engine, lib/placard.ts is a pure domain function (suggestPlacard), lib/supabase.ts handles the remote client and auth, components/ owns UI only.
architecture.modularity: Well-modularized: app/ (Next.js routes), components/ (wizard, map, ui, guide, result, onboarding, building sub-directories), lib/ (domain logic, types, catalog, store), supabase/ (migrations). Boundaries are respected.
notable_patterns: IndexedDB-first with Supabase as eventual sync target (queue + backoff)
Idempotent upserts via client_uuid used as server PK — safe to replay
PostGIS triggers auto-populate geom column from lat/lng client payload
SECURITY DEFINER SQL helper functions to avoid RLS recursion (is_coordinator, owns_inspection)
Service worker strategies: NetworkFirst for navigation, CacheFirst for static assets and OSM tiles, SW version-based cache busting
Placard suggestion is a pure testable function with explicit ATC-20 logic for essential buildings
Gemini analysis marked explicitly as advisory — inspector always has final override with required justification field

code_organization.directory_structure: Logical and clean: app/ for routes, components/ with domain sub-directories, lib/ for pure domain logic, supabase/ for migrations. No clutter.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — TypeScript types, Dexie table names, SQL column names, and enum values all stay in tight alignment (e.g., Spanish enum values match Postgres CHECK constraints 1:1 by design, documented in types.ts).
code_organization.documentation_quality: Inline comments are thorough and purposeful throughout lib/ and the SQL migration (explains RLS intent, backoff rationale, trigger purpose). README covers setup, deployment, and architecture concisely. CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md present for AI-assisted development context.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is consistently well-structured for a hackathon submission: deliberate separation between local and remote layers, domain logic isolated from UI, and meaningful comments that explain non-obvious choices rather than just paraphrasing code.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Gemini API key is server-side only (Next.js API route, process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY never exposed to client). Supabase anon key is public by design (NEXT_PUBLIC_) and is safe per Supabase's RLS model. No secrets in git.
production_readiness.security_notes: Comprehensive Supabase RLS on all six tables with SECURITY DEFINER helpers to prevent recursion. Security headers set in next.config.ts (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy). Anonymous auth by default (zero friction), optional email OTP for coordinators. Storage bucket is intentionally public for emergency access — a conscious trade-off.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: PostGIS geospatial indexes on buildings and inspections tables. Map pull query is capped at 5000 rows. Offline-first design means server load is batched and deferred. Supabase handles connection pooling. No rate limiting on the AI analysis endpoint — could be abused.
production_readiness.reasoning: Auth, RLS, env management, and error handling in the sync queue (backoff, error state persistence) are solid. The missing CI/CD pipeline and complete absence of automated tests are the main gaps for true production readiness. The AI endpoint has no rate limiting or input size validation beyond checking for presence of imageBase64.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — 8,500+ LOC with genuine domain logic: placard algorithm, sync queue, SQL schema with PostGIS triggers, RLS, custom service worker, Dexie schema, AI integration. Not a scaffold or tutorial clone.
maturity.reasoning: Created and pushed on the same day as the hackathon deadline (June 29, 2026) with only 6 commits and 1 contributor. Despite the very fresh history, the code is substantive and the architecture is clearly thought through. Zero tests and no CI are the main maturity deficits.

viability.reasoning: Directly addresses post-earthquake structural triage for Venezuela — the placard algorithm follows ATC-20 (the international standard used by real emergency inspectors), is adapted to Venezuela's COVENIN 1756, and the offline-first design is critical for disaster zones with degraded connectivity. Already deployed to app.reestructuramos.com. The collaborative damage map and re-inspection tracking add operational value beyond a single-device tool. The main risk is lack of tests for the placard logic that will guide access decisions with life-safety implications.

key_features: Offline-first structural inspection wizard (ATC-20 / COVENIN 1756)
Automatic green/yellow/red placard suggestion with inspector override + mandatory justification
Custom sync queue with exponential backoff — works on iOS Safari (no Background Sync API dependency)
Gemini Vision AI crack/damage classification (server-side, advisory only)
GPS-tagged inspections with offline OpenStreetMap tile cache
Collaborative damage map showing all synced inspections with placard color coding
Annotatable photo capture with crack measurement tool
Inclinometer and compass using device sensors
PDF report export + QR code linking to inspection record
Re-inspection support with previous placard carry-forward
Anonymous auth by default; coordinator role via email OTP
Full RLS with PostGIS geospatial indexes and SECURITY DEFINER helpers
Inspector onboarding quiz with ATC-20 knowledge validation
analysis.merge_potential: Moderate-to-high. The collaborative damage map (geo-tagged placard data) could feed a city-wide shelter or aid-logistics layer. The inspector role/credential system could integrate with a volunteer-coordination platform. The Supabase schema exposes inspections with lat/lng and placard status — queryable by any coordinating tool with coordinator-level access.
red_flags: Zero automated tests — placard algorithm makes life-safety access decisions but is entirely untested
No CI/CD pipeline — no build verification or linting gate before deploy
Only 6 commits, 1 contributor — bus-factor of 1 with no review history
AI analysis endpoint (/api/analizar-grieta) has no rate limiting, no input size cap on imageBase64, and no authentication — could be abused or cause unexpected Gemini costs
Storage bucket photos are publicly accessible by URL — acceptable for emergency scenarios but PIDs/location data in filenames are exposed
pullMapData silently swallows all errors with an empty catch — failures are invisible to operators

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Built explicitly for the M7.2+M7.5 Venezuela earthquakes of June 24, 2026. Post-earthquake structural triage (deciding which buildings are safe to re-enter) is a genuine life-safety bottleneck: too few certified inspectors, degraded cell networks, and no standard digital tool for volunteers. Reestructuramos directly fills this gap by putting the ATC-20 placard methodology (the international standard actually used by civil protection agencies) into a mobile tool that works fully offline. The offline-first design — storing all data in IndexedDB and syncing when connectivity returns — is specifically calibrated to the disaster scenario where cell towers are overloaded or down. The problem is concrete, acute, and the solution matches it precisely.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The app loads at https://app.reestructuramos.com and shows a real PWA shell: four-tab navigation (Mapa / Nueva / Mías / Guía), the green/yellow/red placard legend, an onboarding link, and an offline tile-preparation option. The Leaflet map showed "Cargando mapa…" — this is expected behavior in a headless WebFetch evaluation since Leaflet requires full JavaScript execution to render tiles. The app is almost certainly functional in a real browser. The navigation and PWA structure confirm a genuine, deployed product — not a landing page or placeholder.

diffusion.recommended_angle: When the earthquake hit Venezuela, the cell network went down — and that is exactly when Reestructuramos shines. It is the first PWA that lets volunteer inspectors triage thousands of buildings with ATC-20 green/yellow/red placards, GPS-tagged photos, and a collaborative damage map, all 100% offline. Built in hours for the real M7.2+M7.5 disaster of June 24, 2026.
gaps: No real-user testimonials or adoption metrics yet (launched same day as hackathon deadline)
No device screenshots showing the actual inspection wizard in action
Demo video is a YouTube Short — a longer walkthrough showing offline mode, PDF export, and the damage map would strengthen the promotion case
No endorsement or co-sign from FUNVISIS, Protección Civil, or Colegio de Ingenieros yet

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Reestructuramos puts ATC-20 earthquake inspection power in every volunteer's pocket — green, yellow, or red placard triage with GPS-tagged evidence, AI crack analysis, and a collaborative damage map that works even when the cell network is down.

name: ResQLink
db_description:
ResQLink AI es una plataforma gratuita de respuesta humanitaria diseñada para situaciones de emergencia sísmica. Permite conectar en tiempo real a personas que necesitan ayuda con voluntarios, organizaciones y recursos disponibles.

La plataforma utiliza inteligencia artificial para priorizar incidentes, detectar reportes duplicados y recomendar recursos automáticamente. Todo funciona desde el navegador, sin necesidad de instalar nada.

### ¿Qué puedes hacer con ResQLink AI?

- Reportar emergencias con ubicación GPS automática
- Ofrecer recursos como alimentos, agua, medicinas o transporte
- Visualizar un mapa en tiempo real con todos los incidentes y recursos
- Coordinar voluntarios y asignar tareas desde el centro de operaciones
- Consultar estadísticas actualizadas en el dashboard

> **Consejo:** ResQLink AI funciona como PWA (Progressive Web App). Puedes instalarla en tu celular desde el navegador para usarla como una app nativa, incluso sin conexión a internet.

analysis.summary: ResQLink AI is a real-time humanitarian emergency response platform built with Next.js 16 / React 19, Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS), and OpenAI GPT-4o-mini, deployed on Netlify. It is a genuine working MVP — not boilerplate — with verified Venezuela earthquake 2026 incident and resource data pre-seeded, USGS seismic data auto-ingestion, Google News RSS aggregation, AI-powered triage, real-time map (Leaflet/OpenStreetMap with PostGIS), Supabase Realtime subscriptions, and role-based auth. The codebase follows clean architecture (domain/infrastructure/presentation layers). No tests, no CI, and some server-side write operations incorrectly use the anon Supabase key rather than a service role key.

ai_ml: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (priority classification, duplicate detection, summarization, resource recommendation)

architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear: src/domain (entities, enums), src/infrastructure (supabase queries, AI, storage), src/presentation (React components, hooks), src/shared (config, types, utils)

Dynamic import for Leaflet map (SSR-safe)
Middleware-level session refresh + auth-gated routes
Hourly Netlify scheduled function pulls USGS earthquakes and auto-inserts incidents

code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent and descriptive — files, functions, and types follow clear conventions (kebab-case files, PascalCase components, camelCase functions).

The PDF documents (QA Report, Manual, Hackathon Deck) are generated by Python scripts and present a polished facade, but the absence of automated tests means quality assurance is entirely manual.

name: TerraVE
db_description: TerraVE es una plataforma web que combina memoria ciudadana con visión computacional para responder dos preguntas críticas post-desastre: ¿Qué existía aquí? → Levantamiento ciudadano de infraestructura perdida ¿Qué quedó en pie? → Clasificación automática de daño estructural por foto El resultado: un mapa vivo.

analysis.summary: TerraVE is a Next.js 14 App Router web platform that combines citizen-sourced infrastructure memory with AI-powered structural damage classification. Citizens can report infrastructure locations (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) with "before" photos, then upload "after" photos post-earthquake which are sent to a Hugging Face ViT model for automated damage classification. Results are displayed on an interactive Leaflet map with a traffic-light semaphore system. A dashboard cross-references citizen reports with Sentinel-1 SAR satellite data and UNOSAT population exposure data. The app is live at terra-ve.vercel.app with 92 commits. Core flows are implemented end-to-end, but several critical issues exist: the AI model is a generic image classifier (not fine-tuned for structural damage), there is zero authentication, a PATCH endpoint blindly passes user-supplied JSON to the DB, and the dashboard depends on Supabase views (stats_dashboard, poblacion_expuesta, coincidencias_satelital) absent from the committed schema file.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web app (Next.js 14 App Router) — citizen mapping + AI structural damage assessment platform

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router fullstack monolith with serverless API routes
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (BaaS — no ORM; direct table queries via supabase-js v2)
architecture.api_design: REST via Next.js route handlers; pagination implemented in GET /api/lugares; GeoJSON output for map consumption; CORS headers on public data endpoints
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: lib/ for DB/AI/geo utilities, components/ for UI, app/api/ for server endpoints, types/index.ts for shared type definitions
architecture.modularity: Good modularity for a hackathon — classify.ts, supabase.ts, and geo.ts are properly separated; components are single-purpose
notable_patterns: Graceful AI degradation (photo saved even when HF API fails)
PostGIS GIST spatial index on lat/lng for proximity queries
Supabase RLS enabled but fully permissive (open public read/write, intentional for hackathon)
Server client uses anon key (not service role) — operates under RLS as anon user
GeoJSON FeatureCollection output consumed directly by Leaflet map
Haversine distance calculation implemented natively

code_organization.directory_structure: Well-organized: app/ (pages + API routes), components/ (client UI), lib/ (utilities), types/ (shared TS types) — textbook Next.js App Router layout
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming for domain entities (lugares, reportes_dano, color_semaforo) with clear English for infrastructure code; no ambiguity
code_organization.documentation_quality: TerraVE_API.md documents the API, supabase-schema.sql is fully annotated with section headers and comments, inline code comments are meaningful
code_organization.reasoning: Code is clean, idiomatic TypeScript throughout with proper type exports and imports. Minor issues: .gitkeep files scattered in tree indicate scaffolded-first approach, and some components inline Supabase URL/key directly rather than using the lib/supabase.ts abstraction.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Environment variables used correctly (NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix for client-safe keys, HF_TOKEN server-only); no .env files committed; no lockfile present (no package-lock.json or yarn.lock visible)
production_readiness.security_notes: Critical: PATCH /api/lugares/[id] passes raw request body directly to supabase.update(body) with no field allowlist — any client can overwrite any column. No rate limiting on classify endpoint (HF API costs money per call). No file size or MIME type validation on image uploads. Recursive retry on HF 503 could stack overflow under load. All storage buckets and DB tables are fully open to public writes.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Vercel serverless + Supabase scales horizontally without config. Pagination implemented for lugares list (1000-row pages). PostGIS indexes in place. No caching layer beyond HTTP Cache-Control headers. Image uploads as base64 in JSON body could hit Vercel's 5MB body limit for large images.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app has solid error handling (try/catch in every API route with meaningful status codes) and graceful AI degradation, but zero authentication, a raw-body PATCH vulnerability, no tests, no CI/CD, and missing lockfile make it unsuitable for production without hardening.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — substantial business logic: full damage classification pipeline, pagination, GeoJSON conversion, Haversine distance, semaphore mapping, HF API integration with retry. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 92 commits and a live deployment demonstrate real effort, but there are zero tests, no CI/CD pipeline, no Dockerfile, and no lockfile. The dashboard references three Supabase views (stats_dashboard, poblacion_expuesta, coincidencias_satelital) that are absent from the committed schema — unclear if they exist in the live DB or if the dashboard is partially broken.

viability.reasoning: The concept is well-executed and uniquely useful — combining citizen memory (pre-earthquake infrastructure) with AI post-damage classification and satellite SAR data is a strong differentiator. Core citizen reporting and map flows work end-to-end. The fatal weakness is the AI model: google/vit-base-patch16-224 is a generic ImageNet classifier, not trained on structural damage, so 'sin_dano' vs 'destruido' outputs will be unreliable. The label-mapping hack (checking for 'ruins', 'house' etc. in ViT labels) is fragile. Replacing with a damage-specific model (xView2, RescueNet fine-tune) would significantly improve real-world utility.

key_features: Citizen infrastructure registry with before-photo upload (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets)
AI-powered structural damage classification via Hugging Face ViT with graceful fallback
Traffic-light semaphore damage visualization (green/yellow/orange/red) on interactive Leaflet map
Satellite SAR damage zone overlay (Sentinel-1 integration via dano_satelital table)
Population exposure dashboard cross-referencing UNOSAT data by municipality (MMI intensity zones)
PostGIS spatial indexing for proximity queries
GeoJSON API for map consumption
Google Maps geocoding for address-to-coordinate conversion
Venezuela bounding-box validation for geo inputs
Photo storage in Supabase Storage with public CDN URLs
analysis.merge_potential: Medium — the citizen-reporting and map infrastructure is solid and deployable quickly. The structural damage AI is the weakest link and needs a domain-specific model swap before results can be trusted. The PATCH endpoint security hole must be fixed before any public launch. Dashboard views need to be committed to the schema or verified as existing in production.
red_flags: PATCH /api/lugares/[id] accepts arbitrary JSON body and passes it directly to supabase.update() — no field allowlist, allows overwriting any column
AI model (google/vit-base-patch16-224) is a generic ImageNet classifier not trained on structural damage — damage classifications will be unreliable in real use
Recursive retry on HF 503 in classifyDamage() has no depth limit — can cause stack overflow under sustained load
Three Supabase views (stats_dashboard, poblacion_expuesta, coincidencias_satelital) queried in dashboard are absent from supabase-schema.sql — dashboard may be partially non-functional
No rate limiting on /api/classify — any user can trigger costly HF API calls repeatedly
No authentication — completely open public writes with no spam or misinformation protection
No lockfile (package-lock.json/yarn.lock) committed — builds are not reproducible
No file size or MIME-type validation on base64 image uploads — potential for abuse

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Venezuela's earthquake left responders without reliable pre-disaster infrastructure records and no fast way to assess which buildings survived. TerraVE directly addresses both gaps: citizens can register schools, clinics, markets, and pharmacies with "before" photos, and post-quake photos get AI-classified for structural damage. The Sentinel-1 SAR satellite overlay and UNOSAT population exposure data cross-referencing are real tools used by humanitarian organizations. The two-question framing ("What existed here?" / "What remained standing?") maps precisely to the two phases of disaster response: needs assessment and damage triage. The problem is real and the approach is differentiated.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The landing page at terra-ve.vercel.app loads and presents the value proposition clearly, but all statistics show placeholder dashes ("—") indicating no live data is connected. Inner routes /mapa and /reportar both return HTTP 404, meaning the core features (interactive map, citizen reporting form, damage classification) are unreachable in the public demo. The site presents as a concept page rather than a working product from a user perspective, despite the codebase containing 92 commits and substantial business logic. The live demo cannot be demonstrated to a victim or donor today.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the two-question framing — 'What existed here? / What remained standing?' — it is the strongest, most emotionally resonant communication hook and maps directly to humanitarian needs assessment. If the team can fix the routing and seed even a handful of demo data points, a map screenshot showing the traffic-light semaphore overlay would be a compelling visual asset for social posts.
gaps: Inner app routes (map, reporting, evaluation) return 404 — core product is not publicly accessible
All dashboard statistics show placeholder dashes — no real or demo data visible
No demo video showing the end-to-end flow
No screenshots of the working map with semaphore markers
AI damage classifier uses a generic ImageNet model (google/vit-base-patch16-224), not a structural-damage-specific model — real-world accuracy will be poor
Zero real users or field validation
Dashboard depends on three Supabase views absent from committed schema — may be broken in production

evaluation.one_line_pitch: TerraVE maps what Venezuela lost and what survived — citizens document pre-earthquake infrastructure, AI classifies post-quake structural damage from photos, all overlaid with satellite SAR data on an interactive map.

name: Venemed
db_description:
VeneMed — Coordinación de donaciones para centros de salud, con orden y a tiempo.

Hospitales, refugios y centros de acopio reciben donaciones de forma descoordinada. Muchos insumos se desperdician porque nadie tiene visibilidad real de qué se necesita. El resultado es doble: recursos comunitarios desperdiciados y centros colapsados que ya no pueden procesar más ayuda.

Cómo funciona: Los centros publican solicitudes con ventanas cortas de tiempo (12, 24 o 48 horas) indicando exactamente lo que necesitan. Los donantes navegan la lista sin login: ven en tiempo real lo que cada centro está pidiendo y por cuánto tiempo. Esa información es compartible en redes, para que más gente sepa dónde llevar ayuda antes de que la ventana cierre. Si un centro entra en saturación, puede publicar un aviso de exceso para frenar donaciones de un ítem específico.

Para quién. El centro de salud (acceso por WhatsApp, panel propio para crear y gestionar solicitudes) y el donante (acceso público, sin registro). Pensado mobile-first y para funcionar con conexión inestable.

Objetivo. Que la ayuda llegue donde y cuando hace falta — y que ningún recurso comunitario se pierda en el camino.

analysis.summary: VeneMed is a time-windowed medical supply matching platform connecting Venezuelan health centers with donors. Centers register via phone OTP, pass an admin moderation queue, and the public donor feed shows their active "need" or "surplus" requests with configurable expiry windows. An automated cron job expires overdue requests. Built with Next.js App Router, Drizzle ORM, and Supabase Auth. Deployed live at venemed.vercel.app. The code quality is exceptionally high for a hackathon — atomic transactions, TOCTOU race condition handling, JWT-verified auth, audit trail, and tag-based cache invalidation. Critical gap: center-authored request creation is explicitly deferred to "slice 2" and currently 404s, meaning health centers cannot self-serve post new supply needs yet.
analysis.project_type: Next.js full-stack web application (App Router + Server Actions)

infra_deploy: GitHub Actions (CI + e2e + scheduled cron every 5 min)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router with route groups: (public) donor feed, (center) health center back-office, (admin) moderation queue
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM over postgres-js; Supabase used exclusively for Auth (phone OTP); RLS is explicitly bypassed — all data access goes through Drizzle with server-side authz (requireAdmin / requireCenter)
architecture.api_design: Server Actions for all mutations; RSC page components for reads; single secured REST endpoint for the cron expiry job (/api/cron/expire-requests, Bearer token)
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent layering: src/db/(schema, queries, admin-queries, jobs, index), src/lib/auth/(require-admin, require-center, current-center), src/lib/registro/validation (isomorphic), Server Actions co-located per route group
architecture.modularity: High — server-only markers on data modules, shared isomorphic validation module (no Zod by design), explicit type exports, unstable_cache with fine-grained tag invalidation per entity
notable_patterns: Atomic transactions with audit trail for every moderation action and cron expiry
TOCTOU race condition closed via DB unique index on membership(user_id) + catch(23505) reroute
JWT-verified getUser() (not getSession()) used at every auth gate
Cron endpoint fail-closed: missing CRON_SECRET env var blocks the request rather than allowing open access
unstable_cache keyed on normalized filter JSON with revalidateTag on mutation
Denormalized city + categories array on request row for efficient donor feed queries without extra joins

code_organization.directory_structure: Exemplary: route groups (public)/(center)/(admin) mirror product surfaces; src/db/ is cleanly split into schema, queries, admin-queries, jobs; src/lib/ has auth/, geo/, supabase/, registro/; docs/specs/ holds detailed feature specs and a data-model diagram
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent throughout — English identifiers, Spanish user-facing copy, consistent naming conventions across schema enums, query return types, and UI components
code_organization.documentation_quality: Outstanding for a hackathon: inline comments explain design decisions and tradeoffs (TOCTOU, clock authority, cache strategy, OTP rate-limit gotchas, open-redirect prevention); docs/specs/*.md provides product specs per feature slice
code_organization.reasoning: The code reads like a senior engineer wrote it with a deliberate architecture — explicit server-only markers, isomorphic validation module, thorough comment discipline explaining WHY not what. Default README is the only blemish.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: CRON_SECRET loaded from environment and fail-closed (missing secret blocks the request). SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY and DB URLs via Vercel env integration. .env.example documents every required variable with commentary. No secrets committed.
production_readiness.security_notes: Auth via Supabase phone OTP; JWT getUser() (not getSession()) at every gate; admin authorization re-verified in DB (is_platform_admin) on every Server Action; UUID validation on all moderation inputs; phone canonicalized server-side from OTP-verified session (client-supplied phone never trusted). No app-level rate limiting beyond Supabase's OTP limits. RLS bypassed by design (Drizzle + service role) — documented risk.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Stateless Next.js on Vercel scales horizontally. Drizzle uses transaction pooler (port 6543) for runtime, direct connection for migrations. Cron runs on GitHub Actions external to Vercel Hobby plan. unstable_cache with 60s revalidate reduces DB load on donor feed. No queue or worker infrastructure — adequate for expected hackathon load.
production_readiness.reasoning: Live deployment, correct SSR cookie handling, atomic transactions everywhere, secured cron, comprehensive env documentation. Docked one point for no structured logging/observability, no app-layer rate limiting, and RLS entirely absent (Drizzle bypasses it, service role key exposure would be catastrophic).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — substantial domain logic: center registration wizard, admin moderation with audit trail, donor feed with multi-filter search, time-windowed expiry cron. This is not scaffolding.
maturity.reasoning: Three GitHub Actions workflows (CI lint+tsc, e2e with ephemeral local Supabase, scheduled cron). Playwright e2e covers center auth, registration, and admin moderation flows with proper async assertions. However the project is 1 day old, has 1 contributor, 16 commits, the README is default Next.js boilerplate, and the core center request-creation flow is explicitly deferred (currently 404s).

viability.reasoning: Strong domain fit for Venezuelan earthquake relief — medical centers posting supply needs, donors finding and acting on them, admin moderation to prevent abuse, and automated expiry to keep the feed fresh. The donor feed, center auth + registration, admin queue, and cron are all complete and deployed. The critical missing piece is center-authored request creation (explicitly marked 'slice 2; 404 for now' in code), which means health centers cannot self-serve post new needs without direct DB seeding. Given the code quality, adding request creation would take hours not days.

key_features: Public donor feed with city/center-type/supply-category filters and free-text search across center name, city, and supply item names
Phone OTP authentication for health center staff (Supabase Auth + Venezuelan number normalization)
Multi-step center registration wizard with isomorphic validation and TOCTOU-safe DB transaction
Admin moderation queue (approve/reject/suspend) with append-only audit trail per center
Time-windowed supply requests (need or surplus) with configurable window hours and automatic expiry
Automated cron expiry every 5 minutes via GitHub Actions calling a Bearer-secured API endpoint
Modal-based request detail view with parallel route (@modal intercept) for donor UX
Share tracking (WhatsApp/Instagram/X/copy-link) with analytics events stored per request
Center dashboard showing own requests with active/expiring-soon stat tiles
analysis.merge_potential: High — the architecture is clean and well-documented, the live deployment works, and the code quality is production-grade. The main blocker for real deployment is the missing center request-creation UI (slice 2), which is a bounded scope of work given the existing infrastructure. No significant refactoring would be needed to extend it.
red_flags: Center request creation (posting new supply needs/surplus) is NOT implemented — the 'Crear solicitud' CTA links to /centro/solicitudes/nueva which explicitly 404s; health centers cannot self-serve post requests
README is default Next.js boilerplate — no project description, setup instructions, or deployment guide
No Supabase Row Level Security — all data access bypasses RLS via Drizzle + service role key; a service role key leak would expose all data
No application-layer rate limiting on registration or login endpoints beyond Supabase's built-in OTP limits
Cron reliability depends on GitHub Actions schedule (external to Vercel) — if the repo is forked or Actions are disabled, expiry stops working
Single contributor, 16 commits, created June 28 2026 — extremely new, no battle testing

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Medical supply coordination is a critical bottleneck in disaster relief. Hospitals and shelters receive uncoordinated donations — too much of some items, none of others — while donors have no visibility into what is actually needed. VeneMed directly addresses this with time-windowed public requests: centers post exactly what they need and for how long, donors see it instantly without registering, and surplus alerts prevent over-donation. This is not a generic aid platform — it is specifically designed around the asymmetric information problem that wastes resources during emergencies like the Venezuelan earthquake.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The donor-facing feed at venemed.vercel.app is live and working: it shows 2 active requests from "Refugio Casa Esperanza" with countdown timers, social sharing, and filters. The three-step flow is explained clearly. However, the center self-service side (creating new supply requests) is explicitly deferred to "slice 2" and 404s — the CTA "Crear solicitud" is broken. Centers cannot post their own needs yet; current data appears to be seeded. Admin moderation and center registration are functional in code but the end-to-end loop is incomplete for real-world use.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the donor experience: show the live feed with real time-windowed requests, explain that anyone can see what a hospital needs right now — no sign-up required. Emphasize the surplus alert mechanic (prevents waste) and the WhatsApp/Instagram share flow, which is how aid coordination actually spreads in Venezuela. Frame it as 'the missing link between good intentions and actual impact'. Hold a full spotlight until center request creation (slice 2) is shipped — that closes the loop and makes the platform self-sustaining.
gaps: no demo video
no screenshots or visual assets
no branding kit
center request-creation (core supply-side flow) returns 404 — platform cannot operate without seeded data
no real users documented
README is default Next.js boilerplate

evaluation.one_line_pitch: VeneMed shows donors in real time what Venezuelan hospitals and shelters need — with countdown windows so aid arrives before it's too late.

name: Venezuela Juntos (Missing Persons Facial Recognition)
db_description:
## What was built?

  **Venezuela Juntos** is a humanitarian face-matching web app designed to reunite Venezuelan families separated by disaster or displacement.

  ## How it works

Repo: https://github.com/OnBeIt/Venezuela_Juntos_v2

  - Families upload a photo of a missing person with their name
  - Rescuers upload a photo of someone they have found along with a contact phone number or email
  - The system automatically compares faces using AI-powered ArcFace recognition
  - Any pair exceeding the similarity threshold appears on the public Matches page with the rescuer's contact details
  - The app is fully bilingual, Spanish (default) and English

  ## Who is it for?

  Rescuers, volunteers, NGOs, and families searching for loved ones in Venezuelan disaster or displacement scenarios.

  ## Tech stack

  Python · FastAPI · InsightFace (ArcFace) · SQLite · Jinja2 · Tailwind CSS · Docker · Railway

  ## Try it

  Run the app, accept the disclaimer, and use the navigation to report a missing person or register someone who has been found.

analysis.summary: Venezuela Juntos is a FastAPI server-rendered web application for disaster-relief family reunification. Families upload photos of missing persons; rescuers upload photos of people they found with contact info. The app cross-matches faces using InsightFace ArcFace (buffalo_l, 512-d embeddings), stores confirmed matches in SQLite, and publishes them on a public page so families can contact the finder. The ML pipeline is genuinely implemented and functional. Deployment is Dockerized and documented for Railway.
analysis.project_type: Family reunification web app with embedded facial recognition

architecture.pattern: Monolithic server-rendered MVC with async ThreadPoolExecutor for CPU-bound ML
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: SQLAlchemy 2.x declarative ORM over SQLite; 3 tables: MissingPerson, FoundPerson, Match; embeddings stored as raw float32 BLOBs
architecture.api_design: Server-rendered HTML with POST/redirect/GET pattern; no JSON API endpoints; static file serving for uploaded photos via StaticFiles mount
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: main.py (routes only), database.py (models + engine), matching.py (ML orchestration thin wrapper), lib/facerec.py (InsightFace engine with CLI)
architecture.modularity: Minimal but coherent for its size; facerec.py is a reusable standalone toolkit with CLI support independent of the web app
notable_patterns: Model warmup on startup via asynccontextmanager lifespan to avoid first-request timeout
Vectorized cosine similarity using NumPy matrix @ probe (O(N) but in one GPU-less op)
ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) offloads blocking face inference from the async event loop
UUID-prefixed filenames prevent collisions on upload

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat and clean: 4 Python source files at root, templates/ dir, lib/ dir for the AI engine. No unnecessary nesting.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — MissingPerson, FoundPerson, Match, search_pool, embed, warmup, embedding_to_blob are all self-documenting
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually thorough for a hackathon: README documents all routes, DB schema, matching algorithm, env vars, Docker/Railway steps, and known limitations section
code_organization.reasoning: Code reads clearly with no dead code or commented-out blocks. Module boundaries are logical. The README is honest about limitations (no deduplication, no pagination, no admin UI, no notifications), which reflects genuine authorship rather than boilerplate.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets or API keys used; DATA_DIR and INSIGHTFACE_HOME are the only env vars, both documented
production_readiness.security_notes: No authentication on any endpoint — anyone can submit fake missing/found person reports or view all matches. No CSRF protection on forms. File extension is checked but content type is not validated (no magic-byte check). No rate limiting on upload endpoints. The matches page exposes uploaded photos and contact info publicly with no access control.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: SQLite is single-writer — concurrent uploads will serialize or fail under load. O(N) linear scan loads all embeddings into memory on every upload (no vector index). ThreadPoolExecutor capped at 2 workers means at most 2 simultaneous face-recognition requests. 280 MB model held in memory on CPU only. No pagination on /matches could slow rendering as dataset grows.
production_readiness.reasoning: The Dockerfile and Railway deployment docs are solid. However, the complete absence of auth is a serious production gap — in a disaster scenario, bad actors could flood the system with fake reports. SQLite and single-threaded ML inference will not scale past a few concurrent users.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — the facial recognition pipeline (InsightFace, ArcFace embedding, vectorized cosine similarity, DB match storage) is genuinely implemented, not AI-generated scaffolding
maturity.reasoning: 8 commits over ~2 days, zero tests, no CI config. The core ML logic is functional and thoughtfully structured, but the project is clearly a hackathon prototype that has not been hardened for production.

viability.reasoning: The core concept is technically sound and the ArcFace matching pipeline is correctly implemented (L2-normed embeddings, vectorized cosine similarity, configurable threshold). The app is deployable today via Docker/Railway. However, the 0.40 similarity threshold is low and could produce false matches — in a disaster relief context this risk is especially harmful (wrong family reunification). No auth, no rate limiting, and SQLite's write concurrency limit are the main blockers for real-world use at any scale.

key_features: Photo upload for missing persons (family) and found persons (rescuer) with contact info
Automatic face matching using InsightFace ArcFace (buffalo_l) 512-d embeddings on every upload
Vectorized cosine similarity matrix multiply across all opposite-pool embeddings in one NumPy op
Public /matches page showing confirmed face pairs with similarity percentage and contact info
Model warmup at startup (lifespan hook) to preload 280MB model and avoid first-request timeout
CPU-only ONNX Runtime inference offloaded to ThreadPoolExecutor to keep async loop unblocked
Dockerfile with Railway volume instructions for persistent SQLite DB and model cache
DATA_DIR env var for portable data directory (DB + uploads)
analysis.merge_potential: Low — self-contained monolith with no shared API, SDK, or component boundaries; the facerec.py toolkit could be extracted as a library for other projects doing face matching
red_flags: No authentication on any endpoint — fake missing/found person reports can be submitted by anyone
No file content-type validation, only extension check — malformed files could trigger downstream errors
O(N) linear scan loads all embeddings into RAM on every upload — degrades with dataset growth and has no vector index
SQLite single-writer cannot handle concurrent uploads; will serialize or error under real-world load
0.40 cosine similarity threshold (ArcFace default) may be too permissive and produce false-positive matches that mislead grieving families
No CSRF protection on form submissions
No rate limiting on upload endpoints — trivially abused
No deduplication: uploading the same photo twice creates a self-match record
No notification mechanism (email/SMS) when a match is found — families must actively poll the /matches page
ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) hard-caps concurrent face recognition to 2 requests system-wide

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Reuniting families separated during earthquakes or displacement events is a documented, life-critical need. Venezuela Juntos directly addresses it: families upload a photo of a missing person, rescuers upload a photo of someone they found, and the system uses ArcFace AI to automatically match faces and publish contact details. The workflow mirrors real NGO reunification practices (e.g., ICRC's Restoring Family Links). The problem is both urgent and genuine in the Venezuelan context.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: No public web URL — the live_url points back to the GitHub repo. A demo video exists at https://youtu.be/k4PaAmArSfU. The app is Dockerized and documented for Railway deployment, so it can be stood up, but nothing is publicly accessible right now.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the humanitarian mission — AI-powered missing persons reunification for Venezuelan disaster victims — and show the demo video as proof-of-concept. Frame it as 'a team from Germany built this in 48 hours for earthquake victims in Venezuela.' Avoid implying it is production-ready.
gaps: No public live demo for journalists or donors to try
No authentication — anyone can flood the system with fake missing-person reports, which is a serious trust/safety issue for a grieving-families use case
False-positive match risk at 0.40 ArcFace threshold could mislead families, a harmful outcome that undermines credibility
No real users or NGO validation yet
Single contributor, 8 commits, zero tests — very early prototype stage
SQLite and 2-worker ML cap will not survive any real traffic spike after publicity

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuela Juntos uses ArcFace AI to automatically match photos of missing persons with photos uploaded by rescuers — reuniting families separated by disaster in seconds.

name: Venezuela Response Hub
db_description:
## Venezuela Response Hub

Plataforma de coordinación humanitaria construida en respuesta al terremoto de Venezuela (junio 2026), desarrollada durante el ecosistema Build4Venezuela.

### ¿Qué hace?
- Centraliza información verificada de refugios, rutas de evacuación y puntos de ayuda
- Conecta organizaciones humanitarias, voluntarios y afectados en tiempo real
- Integra **SismoLab**, plataforma científica de monitoreo sísmico para Venezuela

### Tecnología
Desarrollado con IA aplicada a coordinación de crisis: procesamiento de datos en tiempo real, geolocalización de recursos y alertas verificadas.

### Impacto
Operativo en horas tras el desastre. Diseñado para escalar a futuras emergencias en América Latina.

### Construido por
Laura Zuluaga- Cofounder & CGO - Young AI Leaders Bogotá Hub · UN AI for Good / ITU Network

analysis.summary: VZLA Response Hub is a bilingual (ES/EN) static-content SSR web app built with TanStack Start that acts as a curated directory hub for Venezuela earthquake response tools. It aggregates ~20 external platforms (people-finders, damage maps, donation channels, shelter maps) with trust ratings, hardcodes real donation bank account numbers, supply drop-off locations across multiple countries, and emergency phone numbers. The site is live at vzlaresponsehub.org. There is no database, no auth, no dynamic data, and no backend API — every statistic and tool listing is a hardcoded string. Built using Lovable.dev (AI-assisted development platform) in under 48 hours of the earthquake, with 306 commits across 2 days.
analysis.project_type: Static information hub / curated disaster-response directory (SSR SPA)

architecture.pattern: Single-route SSR SPA (TanStack Start) with no backend logic or data layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — all content is hardcoded inline in TypeScript source files (i18n dictionary, static arrays)
architecture.api_design: No API routes. server.ts is a thin Cloudflare Worker-compatible handler that delegates to the TanStack Start server entry and normalises catastrophic 500 responses.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Minimal: one 1285-line routes/index.tsx contains all page components, data constants, and UI logic. i18n lives in a separate lib/i18n.tsx file with two large inline dictionaries.
architecture.modularity: Low modularity beyond Radix UI primitives in components/ui/. All domain logic is co-located in the single route file.
notable_patterns: Inline i18n context provider with localStorage persistence and browser locale detection
Cosmetic 'live' animated dot — no WebSocket or polling, purely decorative
Trust-badge system (verified/community/unverified) for curated external tools
Error boundary with Lovable error reporting integration
SSR-safe client-only datetime rendering via useEffect to avoid hydration mismatch

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat and minimal: src/routes/ (1 route), src/components/ui/ (Radix UI wrappers), src/lib/ (utils, i18n, error helpers). No feature folders, no domain separation.
code_organization.naming_quality: Clear and consistent: component names match their purpose (Hero, QuickAccess, Tools, Emergency, Push, Prepare, Trust). Data constants use ALL_CAPS.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is short but accurate. AGENTS.md exists. No inline JSDoc. No changelog.
code_organization.reasoning: The code is readable and well-formatted for a 48-hour hackathon build. The main structural weakness is all domain logic crammed into one 1285-line file. The Lovable.dev origin is evident from import patterns and error reporting hooks.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets required — the app has no API keys, no database credentials, and handles no payments or user data directly.
production_readiness.security_notes: Low attack surface: purely read-only directory site. No user input is persisted. External links use rel='noopener noreferrer'. No CSP headers configured.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Static content served via Vercel edge is inherently scalable. The real operational risk is content staleness: bank account numbers and tool status are hardcoded strings that require a code deploy to update.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is appropriately simple for its use case (a curated directory). Error boundaries and 404/500 pages exist. The hardcoded statistics claiming 'live' data and the cosmetic animated ping dot mislead users into thinking data is real-time when it is entirely static.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real content, AI-generated scaffolding. The tool listings, donation account numbers, and supply drop-off addresses are genuine and appear curated from real sources. However the code was generated via Lovable.dev (evidenced by .lovable/ directory, @lovable.dev/vite-tanstack-config devDependency, and lovable-error-reporting.ts). The 306 commits in 2 days are consistent with iterative AI prompting, not human commit discipline.
maturity.reasoning: No tests, no CI/CD pipeline, no deployment config files found in the repo. The site IS live which is meaningful, but the codebase has no automated quality gates.

viability.reasoning: The hub is deployed, genuinely accessible, and provides real utility as a curated aggregator — it saves users the work of hunting across dozens of emergency tools. The donation section contains verified bank account numbers for recognized organizations (Cruz Roja, Cáritas). The critical limitation for sustained impact is the lack of a CMS: every content update (new tools, changed phone numbers, updated donation accounts) requires a code change and deploy. At hackathon scale this is fine; at sustained disaster-response scale it is a bottleneck.

key_features: Bilingual (ES/EN) with localStorage persistence and browser locale auto-detection
Curated directory of ~20 external humanitarian tools with trust-level badges (verified/community/unverified) and recommended tags
Quick-access layer mapping 4 top-level user intents (find person, need shelter, report damage, donate) to the best tool
Full-text client-side search across all tool titles and descriptions
Donation guide with real bank account numbers for Cruz Roja Venezolana, Cáritas, Colombian Red Cross, and others
Physical supply drop-off point directory for Colombia, Panama, Uruguay, Ecuador, Mexico, Portugal, Spain, and USA
Emergency phone numbers for Venezuelan civil defense, fire, and telecoms providers
Earthquake preparedness guide (during/kit/after) with WhatsApp sharing
Error boundaries with 404 and 500 recovery UI
analysis.merge_potential: Medium. The curation quality and bilingual content are strong assets. The site is already live. However, it is architecturally a pure front-end directory with no data model, so it cannot be merged into a backend-driven platform without a significant rewrite of the content layer. The UI components and i18n pattern could be reused. The curated tool data and donation channel details are immediately portable.
red_flags: All crisis statistics (1430+ dead, 68900+ missing, '3,247 helpers') are hardcoded strings — the 'live' animated indicator is purely decorative with no data source
Social proof testimonials (Dr. Rivas, María from Miami, Andrea from Madrid, Vargas fire team) appear AI-generated and unverifiable
Donation bank account numbers are hardcoded — if any account changes, victims could send money to a wrong or closed account with no mechanism for correction short of a code deploy
Built entirely with Lovable.dev AI code generation — 306 commits in 2 days is an AI iteration pattern, not human authorship
No CI/CD, no tests, no automated quality gates of any kind
No mechanism to add new tools, update tool status, or flag offline tools without a code change and deploy

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The Venezuela earthquake (June 2026) created an immediate information fragmentation crisis: dozens of people-finder tools, shelter maps, donation channels, and emergency numbers appeared overnight with no single entry point. This hub solves exactly that — it curates 22+ verified tools with trust-level badges, maps them to four user intents (find person / need shelter / report damage / donate), provides real bank account numbers for recognized orgs (Cruz Roja, Cáritas), physical supply drop-off locations across 8 countries, and emergency phone numbers. It was live within hours of the disaster. For a non-technical victim or diaspora donor, having one bilingual URL that tells you which tool to use and whether to trust it is genuinely high value.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: vzlaresponsehub.org loads and renders a complete bilingual (ES/EN) crisis directory. Visible sections include: a crisis status dashboard, a categorized tool directory with 22 active platforms, quick-access intent layer, emergency phone numbers, donation guide with bank details, and supply drop-off directory for 8 countries. The site is navigable and information-dense. Critical caveat: the animated "live" ping dot and statistics like "3,247 helpers" and "1,430+ personas fallecidas" are hardcoded strings with no live data source — the "real-time" framing is cosmetic. Social proof testimonials (Dr. Rivas, María from Miami, etc.) appear AI-generated and are not verifiable. The demo video exists at the YouTube link provided.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Built in hours, not days: one bilingual URL that maps 22+ vetted humanitarian tools to what earthquake victims and diaspora actually need — find family, locate shelter, or donate to a verified org right now.
gaps: No screenshots attached to submission for social sharing
Misleading 'live' indicators and hardcoded statistics could backfire if fact-checked by journalists
AI-generated testimonials undermine credibility if surfaced publicly
No real-users signal — zero stars, no community traction yet
Branding is functional but not distinctive enough for viral social posts

evaluation.one_line_pitch: One URL for the Venezuela earthquake: find missing people, donate to verified orgs, and access 22+ vetted humanitarian tools — in Spanish or English, live now at vzlaresponsehub.org.
